text: A customer support agent loop terminates whenever "Issue resolved" appears anywhere in the assistant's response text. During a high-traffic incident, the model begins saying "I'm working to get this issue resolved" mid-task, causing premature termination. What is the architectural fix?
options: Add a confidence threshold: only exit if the model's confidence is above 90%
Change the string match to check only the final sentence of the response
Replace the text-parsing termination check with stop_reason == "end_turn" as the sole loop exit condition
Instruct the model in the system prompt to avoid using the word 'resolved' until the task is complete
explanation: The only reliable completion signal is stop_reason == "end_turn". Parsing natural language for completion phrases is always fragile — phrasing varies across requests and the model has no enforced constraint on when it uses certain words. The anti-patterns to avoid: text parsing, arbitrary iteration caps, and checking assistant content blocks as completion signals. Trust the API's structured signal.
background: Every Claude API response includes a stop_reason field with one of four values: end_turn (model decided it was done), tool_use (model is requesting a tool call), max_tokens (token limit reached), or stop_sequence (a stop sequence was hit). Only end_turn means the model voluntarily finished its task — it is the only correct agentic loop termination signal. Any approach that parses the content of the response to infer completion is fragile by design.

text: A multi-agent research pipeline studying 'AI impact on creative industries' returns reports covering only visual art. Logs confirm: the web-search agent found relevant articles, the document analysis agent summarized them correctly, and the synthesis agent produced coherent prose. The coordinator decomposed the task into: 'AI in digital art', 'AI in graphic design', 'AI in photography'. What is the root cause?
options: The synthesis agent lacks instructions to detect coverage gaps across domains
The coordinator's task decomposition was too narrow — it assigned work that only covers visual art, missing music, literature, and film entirely
The document analysis agent is filtering out non-visual sources based on relevance scoring
The web-search agent's queries are too narrow and should use broader search terms
explanation: Each subagent executed its assignment correctly. The failure is upstream — the coordinator decomposed 'creative industries' only into visual subcategories. Since subagents do not invent scope beyond what they're assigned, the root cause is the coordinator's narrow decomposition. The fix: instruct the coordinator to explicitly map all relevant domains before delegating, or use goal-oriented delegation (target: all creative industries) instead of enumerating subcategories.
background: In a multi-agent system, subagents are bounded by their instructions — they do not autonomously expand scope beyond what the coordinator assigns. This means the coverage of the entire pipeline is capped by the breadth of the coordinator's decomposition. A coordinator that enumerates specific subtasks instead of stating a broad goal transfers its own blind spots directly into the output. Goal-oriented delegation (cover all segments of the creative industry) lets the coordinator or subagent discover and fill gaps dynamically.

text: A financial compliance workflow requires get_customer to run and return a verified customer ID before process_refund is ever called. Production data shows that in 8% of cases, the agent calls process_refund directly using just the customer's name. Strengthening the system prompt has not eliminated the behavior. What is the most effective fix?
options: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt showing the correct sequence: get_customer → process_refund
Rename process_refund to something that implies verification is required first
Use tool_choice: "any" to force the model to call a tool on every turn
Add a programmatic precondition that blocks process_refund from executing until a verified customer ID has been returned by get_customer
explanation: When a sequence has financial or compliance consequences, prompts are probabilistic — they reduce but never eliminate failures. The architectural standard is a programmatic precondition (or PreToolUse hook) that deterministically blocks process_refund until a verified customer ID exists in session state. Few-shot helps calibration but doesn't guarantee order. Renaming is a hint, not enforcement. tool_choice: "any" forces a tool call but doesn't control which one.
background: The Messages API tool_choice parameter can force execution order on a single API call: {"type": "tool", "name": "get_customer"} guarantees the first call is get_customer. For subsequent calls, Claude Code's PreToolUse hooks run deterministically before any tool executes — they can inspect session state and block the call entirely if preconditions aren't met. Unlike prompt instructions (~90% compliance), hooks execute at 100% because they run outside the model inference path.

text: A web-search subagent times out while querying news archives for a multi-agent research task. The coordinator needs to decide whether to retry with a different query, skip the section, or flag it as a gap. The subagent currently returns: {"isError": true, "message": "search unavailable"}. What information must the response include to enable intelligent coordinator recovery?
options: The full stack trace from the timeout exception so the coordinator can debug the root cause
Failure type, the query that was attempted, any partial results retrieved, and suggested alternative approaches
The number of retries already attempted inside the subagent
Only a retry-after delay so the coordinator knows when to try again
explanation: Generic errors hide context. The coordinator needs: (1) failure_type (timeout vs auth vs parsing) to decide retry strategy, (2) attempted_query so it can modify the query on retry, (3) partial_results so it can work with what was retrieved, and (4) alternative_approaches for intelligent fallback. A generic 'search unavailable' forces the coordinator to guess — the result is poor recovery decisions or unnecessary workflow termination.
background: Tool error responses are the primary mechanism by which a coordinator agent can make intelligent recovery decisions. A minimal error (isError: true) tells the coordinator nothing about how to recover. The production pattern: {isError: true, errorCategory: "transient"|"validation"|"permission", isRetryable: boolean, attemptedQuery: string, partialResults: [...], alternativeApproaches: [...]}. Each field enables a specific recovery decision — category → retry or skip; attemptedQuery → reformulate; partialResults → partial processing; alternativeApproaches → fallback.

text: A coordinator agent receives results from a document analysis subagent. A colleague proposes having the analysis subagent send its results directly to the synthesis subagent, bypassing the coordinator. What is the primary argument against this design?
options: Subagents use isolated memory, so direct communication would not carry context correctly
The Batch API does not support direct subagent-to-subagent calls
The coordinator pattern provides centralized visibility, uniform error handling, and control over what information each subagent receives — bypassing it eliminates all three
Direct inter-agent communication requires complex serialization that only the coordinator can handle
explanation: The coordinator is the observability and control plane. When subagents communicate directly: errors become invisible to the coordinator, recovery decisions can't be made centrally, and context leaks between agents in uncontrolled ways. The coordinator controls what each subagent receives — this is how it prevents information overload, enforces task boundaries, and maintains a coherent global view of pipeline state.
background: Claude's multi-agent SDK supports two topologies: hub-and-spoke (coordinator controls all routing and communication) and peer-to-peer (agents communicate directly). Hub-and-spoke is the production default for any pipeline where observability, error recovery, and controlled information flow matter. The coordinator's role is not just routing — it filters what each subagent receives, aggregates results, handles errors, and maintains global pipeline state. Bypassing it removes all these capabilities simultaneously.

text: A coordinator delegates research to a web-search subagent with instructions: 'Step 1: search for X. Step 2: open the first result. Step 3: extract Y.' The subagent frequently misses relevant tangential sources and fails on emerging topics not covered by the scripted sources. What is the correct delegation approach?
options: Provide the subagent with a list of approved URLs to search within
Specify research goals and quality criteria (coverage breadth, source recency) rather than step-by-step procedures — let the subagent determine its own search strategy
Add more steps to the instructions to cover additional source types and edge cases
Reduce the number of steps to reduce cognitive load on the subagent
explanation: Procedural micromanagement creates rigid behavior that fails when conditions don't match the script. Goal-oriented delegation gives the subagent what it needs to succeed: the target output (comprehensive coverage), quality criteria (recency, breadth), and output format. The subagent then applies its own strategy adaptively. Prescribing steps transfers your own blind spots into the subagent's behavior — it can only execute what you listed.
background: Subagents in Claude's agentic framework are capable of self-directing their approach when given a clear goal. The anti-pattern is procedural micromanagement — specifying step-by-step actions that encode the coordinator's limited knowledge of what will work. The production pattern: specify the goal (provide comprehensive coverage of X), quality criteria (sources from the last 6 months, minimum 5 domains), and output format. The subagent is then free to adapt its strategy to what it actually finds.

text: A PostToolUse hook is defined on lookup_order. A PreToolUse hook is defined on process_refund to block amounts over $500. The team debates whether to move the $500 rule from a hook to a system prompt instruction to simplify the codebase. What is the correct decision?
options: Move to the system prompt — it's simpler and the model will follow compliance instructions reliably
Keep as a hook — hooks provide deterministic guarantees; prompt instructions provide only probabilistic compliance, which is unacceptable for financial thresholds
Replace the hook with a validation tool the agent must call before process_refund
Move to the system prompt but add a few-shot example demonstrating the $500 rule
explanation: This is the defining distinction: hooks are deterministic (100% enforcement), prompt instructions are probabilistic (typically >90% but never 100%). For financial operations, safety actions, or compliance rules — any failure rate above 0% is unacceptable. The rule is: when failure has financial, legal, or safety consequences, use hooks. Prompts are for preferences and soft guidance. A validation tool still relies on the model choosing to call it.
background: Claude Code hooks (PreToolUse, PostToolUse) are shell commands that execute outside the model inference path — they intercept tool calls at the application layer and can block, modify, or log them. Because they run deterministically regardless of what the model infers, they provide 100% enforcement. System prompt instructions are processed by the model and honored ~90%+ of the time — the residual failure rate, however small, is unacceptable for financial thresholds, compliance rules, or safety constraints.

text: A multi-agent research pipeline has a web-search agent, document analysis agent, and synthesis agent. The synthesis agent frequently needs to verify specific facts — but 85% of verifications are simple (dates, statistics) while 15% require deep investigation. Currently, all verification routes through the coordinator to the web-search agent, adding 2–3 round trips per task. What is the most efficient fix?
options: Batch all verification needs at synthesis completion and return them to the coordinator as a single list
Cache web-search results proactively based on predicted verification needs
Give the synthesis agent a limited verify_fact tool for simple checks; continue routing complex verification through the coordinator
Give the synthesis agent full access to all web-search tools to handle all verification independently
pattern: Principle of Least Privilege for Tools
explanation: This applies least privilege: give the synthesis agent exactly what it needs for the 85% common case (a constrained verify_fact tool), preserving coordinator-mediated paths for complex investigations. Full web-search access breaks separation of concerns — the synthesis agent becomes a de facto search agent. Batching creates dependencies within synthesis (later steps may need earlier verifications). Speculative caching can't reliably predict needs.
background: The principle of least privilege applied to agent tool access: each agent should have access to the minimum tool capabilities required for its role. A narrow verify_fact(claim, source) tool covers simple fact checks without giving the synthesis agent unrestricted web access — which would blur role boundaries and make the pipeline harder to debug. For the 15% complex cases, coordinator-mediated routing preserves observability and specialized handling.

text: Production logs show that requests like 'analyze the uploaded quarterly report' are routed to the web-search agent's analyze_content tool 45% of the time instead of the document analysis agent's analyze_document tool. Both tool descriptions read: 'analyzes content and extracts key information' and 'analyzes documents and extracts key information.' What is the most effective fix?
options: Add few-shot examples to the coordinator showing: 'uploaded report → analyze_document; web page → analyze_content'
Merge both tools into one with an input_type parameter: 'document' or 'web'
Add a pre-routing classifier that detects whether the user references uploaded files or web content
Rename analyze_content to extract_web_results and update its description to explicitly reference web search results and URLs — eliminating the semantic overlap
explanation: Tool descriptions are the model's primary selection mechanism. Overlapping names and near-identical descriptions cause systematic misrouting regardless of other fixes. Renaming and rewriting the description addresses the root cause — removing semantic ambiguity at the interface level. A classifier adds infrastructure without fixing the underlying problem. Few-shot helps calibration but doesn't remove the ambiguity that causes confusion. Merging conflates two distinct capabilities.
background: When Claude selects a tool, it reasons over tool names and descriptions to find the best match for the current context. If two tools have semantically similar descriptions, the model has no principled basis for choosing between them — and selection becomes effectively random. The tool description is the interface contract between tool author and model. Each description must be specific enough that no other tool's description overlaps: state the exact input type, the domain (web results vs uploaded files), and when not to use it.

text: A document analysis agent is given a general-purpose fetch_url tool so it can download documents by URL. Logs show it now downloads search engine result pages to perform ad hoc web search — behavior that should route through the web-search agent. Which fix best addresses the root cause?
options: Add a system prompt instruction: 'Only use fetch_url to download document files, not to search'
Filter fetch_url calls in a hook to block requests to known search engine domains
Replace fetch_url with a load_document tool that validates URLs point to document formats (PDF, DOCX, etc.) — making search behavior impossible rather than discouraged
Remove fetch_url entirely and route all URL fetching through the coordinator
explanation: Principle of least privilege applied to tool design: replace a general-purpose tool with a domain-specific tool whose interface makes undesired behavior structurally impossible. load_document validates URL formats at the tool boundary — a search engine URL fails validation and the agent can't misuse it. Prompt instructions are probabilistic. Domain filtering is a blocklist that can't enumerate all search engines. Removing the tool and routing through coordinator adds latency for a legitimate use case.
background: General-purpose tools (fetch anything, analyze anything) expand the model's capability surface beyond its role. Misuse isn't malicious — the model genuinely infers that a general-purpose tool is the right one for the task. The correct fix is architectural: replace the tool with one whose interface restricts behavior. A load_document tool that validates document MIME types at call time makes the undesired behavior structurally impossible — no blocklist or prompt instruction needed.

text: A procurement agent's check_inventory tool returns {"results": []} in two cases: when no SKU matches (valid empty result) and when the warehouse database times out (access failure). The agent treats both identically and places unnecessary no-stock orders. What is the correct tool response design?
options: Distinguish the two: return {isError: true, errorCategory: "transient", isRetryable: true} for timeouts and {success: true, results: [], count: 0} for valid zero-match queries
Return null for timeouts and [] for valid empty results
Add an internal retry loop — only return after getting a definitive response
Return a warning flag in both cases so the agent always escalates ambiguous inventory results
pattern: Access Failure vs Valid Empty Result
explanation: These are semantically opposite outcomes requiring opposite agent responses. An empty result set means 'query succeeded, no stock exists' — the agent should communicate no availability. A timeout means 'I couldn't check' — the agent should retry or report unavailability of the tool. Conflating them causes wrong decisions. Internal retries hide the distinction from the model. Null vs empty is still ambiguous. The structured error object gives the model the category and retryability it needs to reason correctly.
background: Access failure and valid empty result are semantically opposite: one means the system couldn't answer the query; the other means it answered and the answer is 'nothing found.' Models treat tool results as factual — if both look the same ([]), the model cannot distinguish them. The production standard: success responses always include a structural indicator (success: true, count: N); error responses include isError: true, errorCategory, isRetryable. Never use the same shape for opposite outcomes.

text: A team configures MCP servers for their project. They have a GitHub integration (used by all developers) and a personal Jira experiment (used only by one developer). Where should each server be configured?
options: GitHub in project .mcp.json (version-controlled, available to everyone); personal Jira experiment in ~/.claude.json (user-level, not shared)
GitHub in ~/.claude.json since tokens are personal; Jira in .mcp.json since it will eventually be team-wide
Both in .mcp.json at the project root for consistency
Both in ~/.claude.json to avoid committing credentials to version control
explanation: MCP configuration scope matches usage scope. Project .mcp.json is version-controlled — it makes server config available to all contributors when they clone the repo. It uses environment variable substitution (${GITHUB_TOKEN}) so tokens are never committed. User-level ~/.claude.json is not shared via VCS — correct for personal experiments. The anti-pattern is putting personal experiments in the project config (pollutes the team's toolset) or putting shared servers in user config (new developers miss them).
background: MCP has two configuration scopes: project-level (.mcp.json, committed to VCS, available to all developers) and user-level (~/.claude.json, machine-local, never shared). Secrets are never stored directly — project config uses ${ENV_VAR} substitution so tokens stay in environment variables. The rule: if a tool is part of the team's workflow, it goes in project config. If it's a personal experiment not yet approved for the team, it stays in user config.

text: An agent has access to 18 tools spanning customer management, order processing, inventory, billing, shipping, analytics, and reporting. Logs show frequent wrong tool selections. A senior engineer suggests reducing to 4–5 tools per agent. Why does the number of available tools affect selection reliability?
options: The model's attention is distributed across all tool descriptions — with 18 tools, similar-purpose tools compete for the same activation patterns, increasing selection errors
More tools increase API request size, causing timeouts that manifest as wrong selections
More tools increase system prompt length, causing the lost-in-the-middle effect on tool definitions
The model is rate-limited to calling 5 tools per conversation regardless of how many are defined
explanation: Too many tools per agent reduces selection reliability because the model must disambiguate between many overlapping descriptions simultaneously. The fix: scope each agent's toolset to its role. A customer-facing agent needs get_customer, lookup_order, process_refund, escalate_to_human — not billing analytics or inventory reports. Agents with tools outside their specialization will misuse them. 4–5 focused tools per agent is the production-proven sweet spot.
background: Claude selects tools by attending to all tool descriptions simultaneously in context. With 18 tools, the model must resolve semantic competition between similar-purpose tools (e.g., lookup_order vs get_order_history vs fetch_order_details) on every turn. The cognitive load of disambiguation at this scale reliably degrades selection accuracy. The architectural fix: decompose into multiple specialized agents (customer agent, billing agent, analytics agent), each with 4–5 tools scoped to their domain.

text: A new engineer joins a team that uses Claude Code. On day one, they find Claude ignoring the team's testing standards and architectural conventions. Investigation reveals the conventions are in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md on the tech lead's laptop. What is the root cause and fix?
options: Claude Code requires a .claudeignore file to activate convention enforcement
The conventions should be in the root README.md since Claude reads it automatically
Project-wide conventions belong in the project-level .claude/CLAUDE.md (version-controlled) — user-level CLAUDE.md applies only to one machine and is never shared via VCS
The new engineer needs to manually copy the tech lead's ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md to their own machine
pattern: CLAUDE.md Hierarchy — Project vs User Level
explanation: The CLAUDE.md hierarchy has three levels: user (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md — applies to one person, never shared), project (.claude/CLAUDE.md or root CLAUDE.md — version-controlled, available to everyone who clones the repo), and directory (subdirectory CLAUDE.md for local conventions). Team-wide standards must be at project level in VCS. The most common mistake: a tech lead puts conventions in user-level config and wonders why new engineers don't have them.
background: CLAUDE.md files form a three-level hierarchy loaded by Claude Code at runtime. User-level (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md) applies to one developer's machine and is never committed to VCS. Project-level (.claude/CLAUDE.md or root CLAUDE.md) is version-controlled and automatically available to every developer who clones the repo. Directory-level (subdirectory CLAUDE.md) provides path-specific conventions. Team standards belong at project level — it's the only level that propagates automatically via git.

text: A codebase has React components, REST API endpoints, Terraform infrastructure, and tests co-located throughout. Each area has different conventions. Tests are scattered across many directories. What is the most effective Claude Code configuration approach?
options: Create a separate CLAUDE.md in every directory containing test files
Use .claude/rules/ files with YAML paths: frontmatter — e.g., paths: ["**/*.test.tsx"] for test rules — so conventions load only when editing matching files
Put all conventions in a single root CLAUDE.md and rely on Claude to infer which rules apply
Create one CLAUDE.md per technology: react.md, api.md, terraform.md at the project root
explanation: .claude/rules/ with glob-pattern paths: frontmatter is the correct tool when conventions apply to files spread across many directories. A rule with paths: ["**/*.test.tsx", "**/*.test.ts"] activates only when Claude edits test files — regardless of where they live. This saves context (irrelevant rules aren't loaded) and is more maintainable than CLAUDE.md in every directory. A monolithic root CLAUDE.md loads all conventions always, wasting context on irrelevant rules.
background: Claude Code's .claude/rules/ directory supports YAML frontmatter with paths: glob arrays. When Claude is about to edit a file, it checks all rule files and loads only those whose patterns match the target file path. A single testing-rules.md with paths: ["**/*.test.*"] covers every test file in the repo — regardless of directory depth — without creating per-directory CLAUDE.md files. Rules not matching the current file are never loaded, keeping context lean.

text: A team wants a /security-review slash command that performs intensive analysis — reading dozens of files and producing detailed output — without flooding the engineer's current working context. Where should the command be stored and what skill parameter is critical?
options: In .claude/skills/ as a SKILL.md with context: fork — runs the skill in an isolated subagent so verbose analysis output doesn't pollute the main session context
In root CLAUDE.md as a conditional section triggered by the word 'security'
In ~/.claude/skills/ with no special frontmatter, since security reviews are personal
In .claude/commands/ with a --verbose flag to control output volume
explanation: context: fork is the key parameter: it runs the skill in an isolated subagent context. The skill's verbose output (reading dozens of files, detailed findings) stays in the subagent's context window and only the summary returns to the main session. Without this, an intensive analysis skill bloats the engineer's working context with noise. Storing in .claude/skills/ (project-level) makes it available to the whole team via VCS.
background: Claude Code skills (.claude/skills/*.md) are reusable task templates invoked with slash commands. The critical frontmatter parameter context: fork runs the skill in an isolated subagent — it gets a copy of the current context, executes independently, and returns only its final output to the main session. Without context: fork, all file reads, intermediate reasoning, and verbose findings accumulate in your working context window, degrading subsequent interactions. allowed-tools further restricts the subagent's capabilities to what the skill needs.

text: A CI/CD pipeline runs claude "Review this PR for security vulnerabilities". The pipeline hangs indefinitely waiting for user input. What is the correct invocation?
options: claude -p "Review this PR..." — the -p (or --print) flag runs in non-interactive mode: processes the prompt, prints to stdout, and exits
CLAUDE_HEADLESS=true claude "Review this PR..."
claude "Review this PR..." < /dev/null to redirect stdin
claude --batch "Review this PR..."
explanation: -p (or --print) is the documented non-interactive mode for Claude Code. It processes the prompt, writes output to stdout, and exits without waiting for input. This is the only correct approach for CI/CD pipelines. CLAUDE_HEADLESS is not a real environment variable. --batch does not exist. Redirecting stdin from /dev/null is a Unix workaround that may cause other issues and is not the supported approach.
background: Claude Code is interactive by default — it prompts for clarification, asks for tool call approval, and waits for user input. In CI/CD pipelines, scripts, and GitHub Actions, this interactive behavior causes the process to hang. The -p / --print flag switches to headless, non-interactive mode: Claude processes the prompt, writes output to stdout, and exits with code 0. Combine with --allowedTools or permissions config to control which tools are available during automated runs.

text: An engineer plans to refactor a monolithic payments service into three microservices — a change touching 60+ files with complex boundary decisions. What Claude Code approach should they use?
options: Direct execution incrementally — start with one file and iterate based on what Claude suggests
Planning mode — explore the codebase, understand dependencies, and design the service boundaries before making any changes; get approval before executing
Start with direct execution and switch to planning mode when the complexity becomes apparent
Direct execution with detailed up-front instructions about the target architecture
explanation: Planning mode is designed precisely for this situation: large changes (60+ files), multiple viable approaches (different ways to define microservice boundaries), and architectural decisions (what belongs in which service). Planning mode uses Read/Grep/Glob to explore without side effects, produces an implementation plan for human approval, then execution proceeds with full context. Direct execution on 60+ files without planning risks expensive rework when wrong boundaries are chosen mid-implementation.
background: Claude Code's planning mode restricts Claude to read-only tools (Read, Grep, Glob) during the exploration phase — no file edits can occur. Claude maps the codebase, identifies dependencies, proposes service boundaries, and outputs a structured implementation plan. The human reviews and approves the plan before any code is written. This separation of analysis from execution is critical for large refactors where the cost of a wrong early decision compounds across dozens of files.

text: A Claude Code PR review pipeline re-runs on every new commit. After a developer pushes 3 more commits addressing the first review's findings, the re-review flags the same issues that were already fixed, creating confusion. What is the correct design?
options: Include the prior review results in context when re-running, and instruct Claude to report only new issues or findings not yet resolved
Start a completely fresh Claude instance with no prior review context for each re-run
Use the same Claude session that generated the original review to ensure continuity
Lower the confidence threshold so previously-flagged patterns don't get re-flagged
explanation: For iterative PR reviews, prior review results must be included as context so Claude knows what was already reported. The instruction 'report only new or unresolved issues' prevents re-flagging fixed items. The anti-pattern is using the same session that generated code — that session retains the generation reasoning and is less likely to critically evaluate its own decisions. A completely fresh context without prior review history reproduces all previous findings regardless of fixes.
background: PR review pipelines run on a loop: review → developer fixes → re-review. Without prior review context, each run is blind to what was already found and fixed. Passing prior findings as context enables differential review: Claude sees what was previously flagged, notes what the new commits changed, and reports only genuinely new or unresolved issues. The self-review anti-pattern (using the same session that wrote the code) is equally important: the code-generation session has strong priors toward its own decisions.

text: A code review pipeline flags comments as 'inaccurate' at a high false-positive rate, eroding developer trust. The current instruction: 'Check code comments for accuracy. Be conservative — report only high-confidence findings.' How should the prompt be improved?
options: Replace 'conservative' with 'very conservative' and add 'only flag obvious inaccuracies'

Define explicit criteria: flag ONLY when (1) a comment describes behavior that contradicts actual code, (2) a comment references a non-existent function or variable, (3) a TODO/FIXME refers to a bug already fixed. Provide DO NOT flag examples (style, missing comments)
Reduce the scope — analyze only security-relevant comments to lower false positive volume
Add a confidence score field and automatically suppress findings below 0.8
explanation: Vague instructions like 'be conservative' leave the definition of 'inaccurate' to the model's interpretation — leading to inconsistent results and false positives. Explicit criteria with category definitions and negative examples (what NOT to flag) give the model precise decision boundaries. This is more effective than hedging qualifiers because it eliminates interpretive ambiguity. The exam principle: concrete categorical rules outperform adjective-based hedges.
background: Prompt precision is the primary determinant of structured output consistency. Qualitative modifiers (be conservative, be careful, only obvious cases) are interpreted differently on every request — the model has no stable reference point for what 'conservative' means in this context. Explicit categorical criteria (flag only when X, Y, or Z; never flag A, B, C) define decision boundaries precisely. Negative examples (what NOT to flag) are often more effective than positive examples because they directly target the false-positive failure mode.

text: An extraction pipeline processes conference registration forms. The attendee_count field is required in the JSON schema. Some forms don't mention attendance figures. The model returns values like '250' or '500' for these forms even at temperature 0. What is the correct fix?
options: Change attendee_count to type: ["integer", "null"] in the schema AND add explicit prompt instruction: 'If attendee_count is not directly stated in the text, return null'
Set temperature to 0.01 instead of exactly 0 to prevent rounding artifacts
Remove attendee_count from the required fields only
Add post-processing regex validation that rejects values not found verbatim in the source text
explanation: Two complementary fixes are needed: (1) making the schema field nullable (type: ["integer", "null"]) so null is a valid output, and (2) explicit prompt instruction to return null when the value isn't in the source. Temperature 0 doesn't prevent hallucinations — it just makes them deterministic. Models hallucinate plausible values for absent fields unless explicitly instructed to return null. Schema-only change without the prompt instruction isn't sufficient — the model needs both the permission and the instruction.
background: When a JSON schema field has type integer and is required, the model interprets this as 'I must return a number.' With no source value to extract, it generates a plausible one. This is schema-driven hallucination — not a reasoning failure. The fix has two parts: (1) change the schema to type: ["integer", "null"] so null is structurally valid; (2) add an explicit prompt instruction: 'If X is not directly stated in the document, return null.' Temperature 0 only makes hallucinations deterministic — it does not prevent them.

text: A structured data extraction pipeline validates output with Pydantic. On validation failure, it appends the error to the prompt and retries. After 5 retries, a legal document extraction is still failing because the source document lists authors as 'Smith et al.' referencing a supplementary appendix that wasn't provided. What is the correct behavior?
options: Return a partial result with 'Smith et al.' as the author value and mark confidence as low
Recognize this as missing information (not a formatting error) and fail fast — automated retry cannot generate information structurally absent from the provided source
Switch to a larger model with greater context capacity
Increase the retry limit to 15 — complex cases require more attempts
pattern: Retry Limits — Missing Information vs Formatting Error
explanation: Retry-with-feedback resolves formatting errors (malformed JSON, wrong data types, arithmetic mismatches) in 2–3 attempts. It is ineffective for missing information — the information simply isn't in the provided context. Retrying 15 times won't create an appendix that wasn't supplied. The correct pattern: classify the error type before retrying. If the error is 'required information is in an external document not provided,' fail fast and flag for manual handling. Partial results with low confidence are a reasonable secondary output.
background: Retry-with-validation-feedback works by giving the model specific error information that lets it self-correct. This is effective for formatting errors — the model has the correct information but output it in the wrong format. It is entirely ineffective for missing information — no amount of retrying can cause the model to produce information that isn't in its context window. Before entering a retry loop, classify the validation error: format/type errors → retry (resolves in 2–3 attempts); missing required information → fail fast and route to human review.

text: A team needs to process 80,000 documents monthly: 200 are urgent regulatory submissions requiring <30 minute turnaround; the remaining 79,800 are standard reports with a 48-hour deadline. A manager proposes using the Message Batches API for all documents to save 50%. How should you evaluate this?
options: Approve only if you implement polling every 5 minutes to catch early batch completions
Reject — the Batch API has no multi-turn tool calling support, which is needed for all documents
Approve — even urgent documents can be prioritized in the batch queue
Partially approve — route urgent submissions to the synchronous Messages API; route standard reports to the Batch API for 50% savings on 99.75% of volume
explanation: The Batch API has no guaranteed latency SLA — processing can take up to 24 hours. This makes it unsuitable for any workflow where someone is waiting for the result. The 200 urgent documents (<30m SLA) require the synchronous API. The 79,800 standard reports (48h deadline) have ample margin for batch processing — 50% cost savings on 99.75% of volume is significant. The multi-turn tool calling limitation matters only if your pipeline requires it — single-document extraction doesn't.
background: The Message Batches API processes requests asynchronously with a 50% cost reduction. Key constraints: no guaranteed latency (up to 24h processing time), no multi-turn tool calling within a single batch item, results retrieved by polling or webhook. The routing rule: batch for any workflow where the caller can tolerate asynchronous delivery; synchronous API for any workflow where the caller or user is waiting for the response. Identify SLA boundaries first, then route accordingly.

text: A multi-agent report pipeline has a Financial Agent (structured JSON tables), News Agent (prose summaries), and Patent Agent (structured lists) feeding a Synthesis Agent. The synthesis output is inconsistent and source attributions are frequently lost. What architectural fix addresses both problems?
options: Insert a format conversion layer between agents and synthesis that normalizes all outputs to a common intermediate representation: {claim, evidence, source, confidence}
Run the Synthesis Agent three times and use majority-vote to select the best output
Give the Synthesis Agent direct access to all three agents' raw data sources for independent verification
Have each agent produce both its native format and a plain-text summary; let Synthesis choose
explanation: Heterogeneous output formats (tables, prose, lists) force the Synthesis Agent to parse multiple representations while tracking attributions — a reliable source of quality degradation. A format conversion layer standardizes everything to a structured schema before synthesis. This: (1) preserves attributions structurally as explicit fields, (2) gives Synthesis a consistent interface, and (3) enables the citation rule: subagents output claim-source mappings that the synthesis agent is instructed to preserve. Direct source access adds latency. Triple-running is expensive and doesn't fix attribution loss.
background: When a synthesis agent receives heterogeneous inputs (tables, prose, lists), it must simultaneously parse multiple formats, maintain attribution chains, and produce coherent output. This cognitive load reliably degrades quality and loses source references. A format conversion layer is an intermediate step that transforms all agent outputs into a common schema — typically {claim, evidence, source, confidence} — before the synthesis agent sees them. Each subagent is required to output claim-source mappings; the synthesis agent is instructed to preserve them. Attribution is now structural, not implicit.

text: A code review pipeline runs a single-pass review of a 14-file PR. Results are inconsistent: some files get detailed analysis, others get superficial comments, and the same anti-pattern is flagged in one file but approved in identical code in another. What is the structural fix?
options: Run three independent full-PR passes and report only findings that appear in at least two runs
Split into two passes: per-file analysis (local issues, consistent depth per file) then a separate cross-file integration pass (type consistency, circular dependencies, data flow)
Switch to a model with a larger context window to process all 14 files without quality loss
Require developers to split large PRs into batches of 3–4 files
explanation: Single-pass multi-file review causes attention dilution — the model distributes attention unevenly across files, producing inconsistent depth and contradictory feedback. The fix: per-file passes ensure each file gets full analytical attention. A separate integration pass then analyzes cross-file relationships (dataflow, type consistency) that per-file analysis misses. A larger context window doesn't fix attention quality — it just fits more content. Three-run majority voting suppresses real bugs by requiring consensus across inconsistent detections.
background: Attention dilution occurs when the model must simultaneously track dozens of files, each with its own context — some files receive more attention than others depending on their position in context. Single-pass review has two distinct failure modes: (1) inconsistent depth across files (attention distributed unevenly), and (2) missed cross-file issues (each file reviewed in isolation). Two-pass architecture separates concerns: per-file passes with bounded context give uniform depth; a separate integration pass with the full diff catches cross-cutting concerns.

text: A customer support agent's session covers a refund inquiry (turns 1–20, resolved), a subscription question (turns 21–38, resolved), and an ongoing payment dispute (turns 39–current). Context limits are approaching. Which strategy preserves the right information at the right cost?
options: Truncate turns 1–20 (oldest) to free space, keeping all recent turns intact
Summarize turns 1–38 (resolved issues) into a narrative description; preserve turns 39–current verbatim as the active unresolved issue context
Start a completely fresh session and pass only the current payment dispute details
Use /compact to compress the entire history equally
explanation: The strategy: summarize resolved issues (freeing tokens while retaining semantic content), preserve verbatim history for the active issue (maintaining reasoning continuity for the unresolved problem). Hard truncation silently loses resolved-issue context that may be referenced later. Starting fresh discards continuity and requires re-establishing customer context. Uniform /compact compresses everything equally — losing specific values from the active issue. The hybrid approach balances token efficiency with conversational coherence.
background: Resolved conversation segments have known information value: their key facts can be captured in a dense summary at a fraction of the original token cost. Active, unresolved segments have unknown future reference requirements — verbatim history preserves nuances that a summary would lose. The hybrid compression strategy: summarize closed issues into narrative descriptions (who, what, outcome), preserve the active issue verbatim. This is the standard pattern for multi-issue customer support sessions approaching context limits.

text: An agent session from 6 hours ago contains tool_result messages showing order status: {"status": "PENDING", "expected_resolution": "24h"}. When the session is resumed and a customer asks for their order status, the agent confidently says 'your order is still pending with about 18 hours remaining.' The order was actually delivered 2 hours ago. What is the architectural fix?
options: Add to the system prompt: 'Tool results from prior sessions may be outdated — always verify'
Resume with full history including old tool_results; the model should infer staleness from timestamps
Store a session_start timestamp and automatically expire tool_results older than 1 hour
When resuming after a time gap, programmatically strip all previous tool_result messages from history — keep only human/assistant turns, forcing the agent to re-fetch all data
pattern: Resuming Async Sessions — Stale Tool Results
explanation: Models treat tool_result content as ground truth — they don't infer staleness from gaps or timestamps. The deterministic fix: strip all tool_result messages before resumption. This forces the agent to re-call every tool, ensuring all data is current. Preserving human/assistant turns maintains conversational continuity without stale facts. System prompt warnings about staleness are probabilistic — the model may still cite the cached status. Timestamp-based expiry is reasonable but the programmatic strip is simpler and more reliable.
background: In Claude's conversation format, tool_result messages are structurally indistinguishable from current data — the model has no built-in mechanism to assess their age. After a time gap, these cached results become false ground truth. The production fix: before resuming a session that spans a time gap, programmatically filter the message history to remove all tool_result blocks while preserving human and assistant turns. This maintains conversational context while forcing fresh tool calls for all data-dependent questions.

text: In a long codebase investigation, the agent extracts a key fact: PaymentProcessor.refund() is called from 3 places. Twenty turns later, when asked 'where is refund() called from?', the agent says 'typically from the order controller, though the exact locations may vary.' The agent clearly 'forgot' the specific finding. What pattern prevents this?
options: Have the agent actively maintain a scratchpad file (e.g., investigation-scratchpad.md) recording key findings — the agent reads this dense structured file for subsequent questions instead of relying on raw message history
Use a larger context window model to retain more session history
Restart the session every 30 minutes with a pasted summary
Use /compact before the session gets too long to prevent context degradation
explanation: Token decay in long sessions causes the model to lose coherence about early discoveries buried deep in history. The scratchpad externalizes memory: as the agent discovers facts, it writes them to a structured file. For subsequent questions, it reads the scratchpad (dense, always current) rather than scanning raw history. This is independent of context window size — the decay is about position, not just volume. /compact compresses history but loses specific numeric values. Restarts lose all prior context.
background: In long agentic sessions, early findings are buried far from the end of the context window. Models' effective recall degrades with distance from the end of context — a phenomenon known as lost-in-the-middle. A scratchpad file externalizes memory: the agent writes structured findings (file paths, function names, call sites, decisions) as it discovers them. For subsequent questions, it reads the dense scratchpad rather than scanning message history. The scratchpad is always at the end of context when read — maximizing recall fidelity.

text: A customer support agent's context shows order status from 3 prior calls to lookup_order, each returning 40+ fields. The agent only needed 5 fields (order_id, status, items, return_window, total) from each call. Context is 60% consumed by irrelevant order data. What is the correct mitigation?
options: Instruct the model in the system prompt to ignore irrelevant tool result fields
Switch to a model with a larger context window
Implement a PostToolUse hook on lookup_order that filters the response to the 5 relevant fields before the model receives it
Use the Batch API to process tool calls asynchronously to reduce inline context usage
pattern: Tool Context Pruning via PostToolUse Hook
explanation: The PostToolUse hook intercepts tool results before they reach the model — it's the correct layer to implement context pruning. Filtering to 5 relevant fields from a 40-field response reduces per-call context cost by ~87.5%. At 3 calls, this transforms a context crisis into a manageable footprint. System prompt instructions to 'ignore' fields don't prevent them from consuming tokens — they're still in context. Larger context windows defer but don't solve the accumulation. Batch API processes requests differently but doesn't affect inline context.
background: Every tool_result message in conversation history consumes tokens proportional to its size — and persists for all subsequent turns. A 40-field API response injected 3 times means those 120 field instances remain in context indefinitely. The PostToolUse hook is the correct interception point: it runs after the tool returns its result but before the result enters the conversation history. The hook transforms the raw response to include only the fields the model needs — reducing per-call context cost by 80–90%. Prompt instructions to 'ignore' fields have no token effect.

text: A data extraction team deploys a pipeline automating processing for extractions with field-level confidence >90%. They validate on 10,000 documents and get 94% aggregate accuracy. Six weeks post-deployment, they find handwritten invoices (18% of volume) have 55% accuracy while printed invoices have 99%. The aggregate was misleading. What deployment validation rule was violated?
options: The team needed more labeled training examples for handwritten documents
Stratified sampling should have been done post-deployment as a monitoring mechanism, not pre-deployment as a validation gate
Accuracy must be validated by document type and field segment — not just overall aggregate — before deploying automation. High aggregate scores can mask catastrophic failure in specific segments
The confidence threshold should have been set higher (95%) before deployment
explanation: 99% on 82% of volume (printed) + 55% on 18% (handwritten) = ~94% aggregate — which looks excellent. But 55% automation accuracy on handwritten invoices is a production disaster. The validation rule: analyze accuracy by document type, field type, and any other meaningful segment before declaring the threshold safe. An aggregate metric is insufficient when the distribution has important subgroups. Stratified validation pre-deployment is the gate; stratified monitoring post-deployment catches drift. The threshold level doesn't matter if it's validated on a misleading aggregate.
background: Aggregate accuracy metrics hide per-segment performance. A system with 99% accuracy on the majority class and 55% on a minority class reports a deceptively high overall number — but the minority class is likely a production disaster. The validation rule: before deploying any automation threshold, compute accuracy broken down by every meaningful subgroup (document type, field type, source format, date range). If any segment falls below your minimum acceptable accuracy, the aggregate threshold cannot be trusted. This is a pre-deployment gate — post-deployment stratified monitoring then tracks drift.

text: In the MCP architecture, which component is responsible for enforcing security policies, handling user authorization decisions, and coordinating AI/LLM integration across multiple client instances?
explanation: The Host is the container and coordinator that creates/manages clients, enforces security policies, handles user consent, and coordinates AI integration. Servers operate independently and cannot enforce cross-cutting security. Clients maintain isolated 1:1 sessions with servers but don't coordinate across each other. There is no separate 'authorization server' role in MCP's core architecture.
background: MCP follows a client-host-server architecture. The Host is the top-level process that creates multiple Client instances, each with a 1:1 relationship to a Server. The Host enforces security boundaries, controls permissions, and aggregates context—servers and clients do not handle cross-cutting orchestration concerns.
options: Each individual MCP Server
The Host process
A dedicated authorization server defined in the MCP spec
The MCP Client that initiated the connection

text: An agent is connected to 15 MCP servers exposing a total of 400 tools. Loading all tool definitions into the context window consumes over 150K tokens per request. Which production pattern addresses this scaling problem?
pattern: Code Execution with MCP — Progressive Discovery
explanation: Anthropic's code-execution-with-MCP pattern has the agent write code that calls MCP server APIs directly, using progressive tool discovery (e.g., search_tools with name-only detail) to load schemas on demand—reducing tokens from 150K to ~2K (98.7% reduction). Increasing output tokens doesn't reduce input context bloat. Splitting across conversations adds orchestration complexity and doesn't solve per-conversation overhead. Prompt caching reduces latency but still bills for the cached tokens and doesn't address the fundamental context window pressure.
background: As MCP adoption scales, loading all tool definitions upfront becomes a bottleneck. The code-execution-with-MCP pattern lets agents discover tools progressively—first searching by name, then loading full schemas only for relevant tools—and filter intermediate data in the execution environment rather than passing it through the context window.
options: Cache all tool definitions in a prompt cache so they are free on subsequent turns
Increase the model's max output tokens so it can process all 400 tool definitions in a single pass
Split the 400 tools evenly across multiple parallel conversations and merge the results
Code-execution-with-MCP: the agent writes code against MCP server APIs, using progressive tool discovery to load only needed tool schemas on demand

text: In the MCP specification, what is the relationship cardinality between an MCP Client and an MCP Server?
explanation: Per the MCP spec, each Client is created by the Host and establishes one stateful session with exactly one Server—a strict 1:1 relationship. The Host creates multiple Clients to connect to multiple Servers, but each Client is isolated. Clients do not share sessions, and sessions are stateful (not stateless) by default, with capability negotiation at initialization.
background: MCP's 1:1 client-server model is a deliberate architectural choice for isolation. Each client handles protocol negotiation, message routing, and subscriptions for exactly one server. The host is responsible for creating multiple clients when multiple server connections are needed, maintaining clear security boundaries between them.
options: Each Client maintains exactly one stateful session with one Server (1:1)
Multiple Clients share a single session with one Server (N:1 shared session)
Each Client can connect to multiple Servers simultaneously (1:N)
Clients and Servers communicate statelessly with no persistent session

text: An MCP server needs to perform a multi-step workflow that takes several minutes. Which feature introduced in the November 2025 MCP spec allows the server to report progress and lets the client query completion status asynchronously?
pattern: MCP Tasks for Async Operation Tracking
explanation: The November 2025 spec introduced Tasks as a first-class feature for tracking async operations with status queries. Elicitation is for servers to request additional information from users, not for progress tracking. Sampling allows servers to request LLM completions, not monitor workflow status. Roots let servers inquire about URI/filesystem boundaries, not track async operations.
background: The November 2025 MCP spec release added support for task-based workflows, enabling servers to represent long-running operations as Tasks. Clients can query task status, enabling async patterns where the client doesn't need to block waiting for completion—critical for production workflows involving multi-step or time-consuming server-side operations.
options: Roots — the server queries the client filesystem for a status file
Sampling — the server recursively invokes the LLM to check progress
Tasks — async operation tracking with status queries
Elicitation — the server requests progress updates from the user

text: According to MCP's design principles, what information does a server receive about the user's conversation with the LLM?
explanation: MCP's design principles explicitly state that servers should not be able to read the whole conversation or see into other servers. They receive only necessary contextual information, with the full conversation staying with the host. Sharing full history would violate isolation. The spec does not mandate host-generated summaries. Cross-server context sharing is explicitly prevented by design.
background: Server isolation is a core MCP security principle. The host process retains the full conversation and enforces boundaries, passing only what's needed to each server. This prevents data leakage between servers and limits each server's exposure to the minimum context required for its function.
options: The full conversation history, so the server can provide contextually relevant responses
A summary of the conversation generated by the host before each tool call
Only the necessary contextual information — servers cannot read the full conversation or see into other servers
The conversation history from all connected servers, enabling cross-server context sharing

text: When an MCP client and server first connect, how do they agree on which protocol features to use during the session?
explanation: MCP uses explicit capability negotiation during initialization: servers declare what they support (resources, tools, prompts) and clients declare theirs (sampling, roots, elicitation). Both must respect declared capabilities for the session. It's not an all-or-nothing requirement. Runtime error-based detection would be fragile and is not the protocol's approach. Static config files are not part of the MCP handshake mechanism.
background: Capability negotiation is part of MCP's base protocol lifecycle. During initialization, the client and server exchange capability declarations. This ensures both sides know exactly which features are available, enabling graceful degradation when certain features aren't supported rather than runtime failures.
options: Through capability negotiation at initialization — both sides declare supported features and must respect them throughout the session
Features are auto-detected at runtime based on which JSON-RPC methods return errors
The host pre-configures a static feature set in a shared configuration file before any connection
The client sends a feature request list and the server must support all of them or reject the connection

text: In the code-execution-with-MCP pattern, what is the primary mechanism that reduces intermediate tool result tokens from consuming the context window?
explanation: In the code-execution-with-MCP pattern, the agent writes code that runs in a sandboxed execution environment. Intermediate data stays in that environment and is processed/filtered there — only the final, relevant result is returned to the model's context. MCP servers don't auto-compress results. Prompt caching doesn't reduce context consumption of intermediate results. Streaming to users doesn't remove results from context.
background: When agents call tools directly, each intermediate result is appended to the context window, quickly consuming tokens. By having the agent write code that executes tool calls and processes results in a sandboxed environment, intermediate data never enters the context window. This is especially impactful for data-heavy workflows like aggregations across multiple API calls.
options: Tool results are automatically compressed by the MCP server before returning them
The model streams tool results directly to the user, bypassing the context window entirely
Data is filtered and processed within the code execution environment, so only final results enter the context window
The host applies prompt caching to all intermediate tool results

text: When using Claude's tool use feature, which three MCP server primitives can a server expose to clients according to the specification?
explanation: The MCP spec defines exactly three server primitives: Resources (context and data), Tools (functions for the AI to execute), and Prompts (templated messages and workflows). 'Functions' and 'Schemas' are not MCP primitives. 'Actions' and 'Templates' are not spec terminology. 'Endpoints' and 'Data Sources' are informal terms not part of the MCP primitive taxonomy.
background: MCP servers expose three distinct primitive types: Resources provide context and data for users or models to consume; Tools expose executable functions the AI model can invoke; Prompts offer templated messages and workflows for users. Understanding these primitives is foundational to designing effective MCP server interfaces.
options: Resources, Actions, and Templates
Resources, Tools, and Prompts
Tools, Functions, and Schemas
Endpoints, Tools, and Data Sources

text: The November 2025 MCP spec introduced a feature that allows servers to securely request additional information from users during a session, without the server having direct access to the user. What is this feature called?
explanation: Elicitation is the MCP client feature that enables server-initiated requests for additional information from users, routed securely through the client/host. Sampling is a different feature where servers request LLM completions through the client. 'User Prompts' is not an MCP term — Prompts are templated messages, not interactive requests. 'Interactive Roots' is not a real MCP feature.
background: Elicitation enables secure out-of-band interactions where an MCP server can request additional user input without having direct access to the user. The request flows through the client and host, which enforce security and consent policies. This is essential for workflows where servers need clarification or user decisions mid-operation.
options: Elicitation
Interactive Roots
Sampling
User Prompts

text: An MCP server exposes a search_tools method that supports three detail levels: name-only, name+description, and full schema. What is the primary benefit of this progressive disclosure approach?
explanation: Progressive disclosure lets agents first search by name (very few tokens), then optionally get descriptions to narrow further, and only load full schemas for tools they'll actually use. This directly reduces context window consumption. Hiding deprecated tools is a versioning concern, not the primary purpose. Host caching by security level is not the design intent. Server memory constraints are not the motivating problem — context window token cost is.
background: Progressive tool discovery is a pattern for scaling MCP tool access. By organizing tools as searchable resources with multiple detail levels, agents avoid loading hundreds of full tool schemas upfront. Instead, they perform lightweight searches, select relevant tools, and fetch complete definitions only as needed — dramatically reducing token usage.
options: It enables the host to cache tool schemas at different granularities for different security levels
It ensures all tool definitions fit within the server's own memory constraints
The agent can discover relevant tools with minimal token cost, loading full schemas only for the tools it actually needs
It allows the server to hide deprecated tools from agents that request only name-only detail

text: You are building a multi-agent system where a primary agent delegates tasks to specialized sub-agents. Each sub-agent connects to different MCP servers. According to MCP's architecture, which component is responsible for enforcing security policies and controlling cross-server interactions?
explanation: The MCP architecture spec states the host process 'enforces security policies and consent requirements' and 'controls client connection permissions and lifecycle.' Servers cannot see into other servers or the full conversation — the host enforces these boundaries. Distractor 1 fails because servers operate independently and don't enforce cross-server policy. Distractor 2 fails because clients maintain isolated 1:1 sessions; the host coordinates across them. Distractor 3 is fabricated — the MCP registry is a discovery index, not a security enforcement layer.
background: In MCP's client-host-server architecture, the host is the top-level coordinator. It creates and manages multiple clients, each with a 1:1 relationship to a server. Security decisions — including user authorization, consent, and cross-server isolation — are the host's responsibility, not the server's or client's.
options: A centralized MCP registry that validates permissions before routing requests
The MCP client, which applies a shared security policy across all connected servers
The host process, which acts as the container and coordinator for all clients
Each MCP server, which self-enforces its own security policies

text: An MCP server exposes both Resources and Tools as primitives. Which statement correctly distinguishes their intended roles in the protocol?
explanation: The MCP specification defines Resources as 'context and data, for the user or the AI model to use' and Tools as 'functions for the AI model to execute.' Distractor 1 inverts the mutability model — resources are primarily contextual data, not read-write stores. Distractor 2 conflates resources with notifications, which are a separate protocol mechanism. Distractor 3 is incorrect because they serve fundamentally different purposes in the protocol.
background: MCP servers expose three primitive types: Resources (context/data), Tools (executable functions), and Prompts (templated workflows). Understanding these distinctions is essential for designing well-structured MCP servers that correctly separate data retrieval from action execution.
options: Resources are read-write data stores, while Tools are read-only query endpoints
Resources provide context and data for the user or model, while Tools are functions the AI model executes
Resources and Tools are interchangeable primitives with different naming conventions
Resources are server-to-client push notifications, while Tools are client-to-server requests

text: In the MCP architecture, a single host application manages three MCP clients. What is the correct cardinality relationship between clients and servers?
explanation: The MCP architecture specification states each client 'establishes one stateful session per server' and has a '1:1 relationship with a particular server.' Distractor 1 is wrong — clients don't multiplex across servers. Distractor 2 is wrong — connections are isolated per client, not pooled. Distractor 3 is wrong on two counts: sessions are stateful, and the relationship is strictly 1:1.
background: MCP uses stateful sessions built on JSON-RPC 2.0. The host creates multiple client instances, each maintaining an isolated connection to exactly one server. This isolation is a core security design principle — it prevents servers from seeing into each other's sessions.
options: Each client establishes stateless request-response connections to one or more servers
Each client maintains exactly one stateful session with one server
Each client can connect to multiple servers simultaneously through multiplexed sessions
Clients share a single pooled connection to all servers managed by the host

text: You need Claude to extract structured data from legal documents that may contain ambiguous or missing fields. The output schema uses an enum field for contract type with values ["NDA", "MSA", "SOW", "OTHER"]. What is the best practice for handling documents where the contract type cannot be confidently determined?
explanation: Best practice for structured extraction is to provide an explicit escape value (like UNKNOWN) in enums for ambiguous cases, paired with clear instructions. This prevents forced hallucination. Distractor 1 violates schema consistency — omitting fields makes downstream parsing unreliable. Distractor 2 forces the model to guess, increasing hallucination risk. Distractor 3 adds unstructured output that defeats the purpose of constrained schemas and complicates parsing.
background: When designing structured output schemas for extraction tasks, every field should have a clear path for the model to express uncertainty without hallucinating. For enum fields, this means including an explicit value like UNKNOWN or UNSPECIFIED. This pattern complements nullable fields by addressing cases where the field is present but ambiguous.
options: Add a free-text field alongside the enum so the model can explain its uncertainty
Instruct the model to always select the closest match from the existing enum values
Omit the contract type field from the output and let the consumer infer it from other fields
Include "UNKNOWN" in the enum values and instruct the model to use it when the type cannot be determined from the document

text: When designing an MCP server, which of the following is a stated design principle in the MCP specification?
explanation: The MCP spec explicitly states 'Servers should be extremely easy to build' and 'Host applications handle complex orchestration responsibilities.' Distractor 1 directly contradicts this — orchestration belongs to the host. Distractor 2 violates the isolation principle: 'Full conversation history stays with the host.' Distractor 3 violates the principle that 'Cross-server interactions are controlled by the host.'
background: MCP's design principles prioritize server simplicity and composability. Servers focus on specific, well-defined capabilities with simple interfaces. The host handles orchestration, security enforcement, and cross-server coordination. This separation of concerns makes servers easy to build and maintain.
options: Servers should maintain a copy of the full conversation history for contextual awareness
Servers should implement comprehensive orchestration logic to reduce host complexity
Servers should coordinate directly with other servers for cross-cutting concerns
Servers should be extremely easy to build, with hosts handling complex orchestration responsibilities

text: An agent processes a 180K-token document and must answer follow-up questions across a multi-turn conversation. After several turns, the context window is nearly full. Which approach best maintains answer quality while staying within token limits?
explanation: Context compaction intelligently summarizes prior conversation turns, preserving essential information while freeing token space. Distractor 1 (naive truncation) risks losing critical context that earlier turns established. Distractor 2 introduces consistency problems — parallel sessions can't reference each other's findings. Distractor 3 doesn't address the core problem and may produce incomplete answers.
background: As multi-turn conversations grow, context compaction becomes essential. Rather than naive truncation, compaction summarizes prior turns to preserve key facts, decisions, and context while dramatically reducing token usage. This is a built-in capability in Claude's API designed for long-running sessions.
options: Truncate the oldest messages from the conversation history to free up token space
Reduce the model's max output tokens to leave more room for the input context
Split the document across multiple parallel agent sessions and merge their answers
Use context compaction to summarize prior turns while preserving the key facts needed for follow-up answers

text: A team wants certain rules to apply only when editing files in their src/api/ directory using Claude Code. Which mechanism provides path-scoped rule loading?
explanation: Claude Code's .claude/rules/ directory supports path-scoped loading via glob patterns, activating rules only when the user is working on matching files. Distractor 1 is wrong — CLAUDE.md doesn't support if-else conditional blocks. Distractor 2 is impractical and breaks the unified workspace model. Distractor 3 is fabricated — environment variables don't control rule loading in Claude Code.
background: Claude Code supports hierarchical configuration through CLAUDE.md files and .claude/rules/ directories. Rules in .claude/rules/ can be scoped to specific file paths using glob patterns, enabling teams to apply different coding standards, review criteria, or instructions depending on which part of the codebase is being edited.
options: Use environment variables to toggle API-specific rules at runtime
Place rule files in .claude/rules/ with glob patterns that match the src/api/ path
Add conditional logic inside the top-level CLAUDE.md using if-else blocks
Create a separate Claude Code workspace for API files with its own global config

text: The MCP specification defines three server primitives. Which of the following correctly lists all three?
explanation: The MCP specification explicitly defines three server primitives: Resources (context/data), Tools (executable functions), and Prompts (templated messages and workflows). Distractor 1 replaces Prompts with Schemas — schemas are part of tool definitions, not a primitive. Distractor 2 replaces Resources with Notifications — notifications are a protocol utility, not a primitive. Distractor 3 uses wrong terminology: Functions and Templates are not official MCP primitive names.
background: MCP servers expose exactly three primitive types to clients: Resources provide context and data, Tools expose executable functions for the AI model, and Prompts offer templated messages and workflows for users. Understanding these primitives is foundational to designing well-structured MCP servers.
options: Tools, Prompts, and Notifications
Resources, Tools, and Prompts
Resources, Tools, and Schemas
Resources, Functions, and Templates

text: An MCP client supports Sampling as a client feature. What does Sampling enable in the MCP protocol?
explanation: The MCP specification defines Sampling as a client feature that enables 'server-initiated agentic behaviors and recursive LLM interactions.' This allows servers to request the client/host to perform LLM inference on the server's behalf. Distractor 1 confuses sampling with tool selection strategies. Distractor 2 confuses sampling with streaming, which is a separate transport concern. Distractor 3 confuses protocol sampling with observability/telemetry sampling.
background: In MCP, Sampling is a client-side feature that lets servers request LLM completions through the client. This enables servers to trigger recursive, agentic behaviors without directly accessing the model. The host maintains control by mediating all sampling requests, enforcing security policies and user consent.
options: The server streams partial results to the client for progressive rendering
The host collects usage telemetry by sampling a percentage of requests
Server-initiated agentic behaviors and recursive LLM interactions through the client
The client randomly samples a subset of available tools to reduce context window usage

text: An agent orchestration system needs to handle a scenario where Claude requests a tool call, but the tool is temporarily unavailable due to a network timeout. What is the recommended production pattern for handling transient tool failures in an agentic loop?
pattern: Graceful Tool Failure in Agentic Loops
explanation: Returning is_error: true with a descriptive message lets Claude reason about the failure and choose an appropriate recovery strategy (retry, use alternative tool, or inform the user). Silent infinite retries risk hanging. Terminating immediately is too aggressive for transient errors. Empty strings mislead the model into reasoning over missing data.
background: In agentic loops, tool execution failures should be reported back to the model as error results rather than handled silently. Claude can then apply its reasoning to decide the best recovery action, which may include retrying, choosing a different tool, or gracefully informing the user.
options: Silently retry the tool call in an infinite loop until it succeeds before returning any result to Claude
Return a tool result with is_error: true and a descriptive error message, allowing Claude to decide whether to retry or take an alternative approach
Replace the tool result with an empty string and continue the loop as if the call succeeded
Terminate the agentic loop immediately and return a system error to the user

text: According to the MCP specification, which transport mechanisms are supported for communication between MCP clients and servers?
explanation: The MCP spec defines two transports: stdio for local process communication and Streamable HTTP for remote services. WebSocket, gRPC, raw TCP, REST, and GraphQL are not part of the MCP transport specification.
background: MCP supports two official transport mechanisms. stdio is designed for local processes where the client spawns the server as a subprocess. Streamable HTTP is used for remote services, enabling communication over standard HTTP infrastructure. The 2026 roadmap addresses scaling gaps with Streamable HTTP.
options: stdio for local processes and Streamable HTTP for remote services
WebSocket and gRPC
stdio and raw TCP sockets
HTTP REST and GraphQL

text: An agent connected to 15 MCP servers has over 800 tool definitions loaded into context. Each API call now costs significantly more tokens before the model even reads the user's request. Which production pattern best addresses this scaling problem?
pattern: Code Execution Token Reduction at Scale
explanation: Code-execution-with-MCP enables agents to write code that calls tools programmatically, loading definitions on-demand rather than all upfront. Anthropic's engineering blog reports up to 98.7% token reduction with this approach. Increasing context window doesn't reduce cost.
