Concatenating descriptions doesn't meaningfully reduce tokens and hurts structured parsing. Removing descriptions entirely would severely degrade tool selection accuracy.
background: As MCP tool counts grow into the hundreds or thousands, loading all definitions upfront becomes a major cost and latency bottleneck. The code-execution-with-MCP pattern has the agent write code against filesystem-organized tool APIs, using progressive discovery (search_tools with detail levels) to load only needed definitions, reducing token usage from ~150K to ~2K tokens.
options: Increase the model's context window size to accommodate all tool definitions comfortably
Concatenate all tool descriptions into a single system prompt paragraph to reduce token overhead
Use code-execution-with-MCP so the agent writes code against MCP server APIs, loading tool definitions on-demand via progressive discovery instead of upfront
Remove tool descriptions entirely and rely on tool names alone for the model to infer usage

text: You need Claude to extract structured data from messy OCR output where some fields may be legitimately absent from the source document versus fields that exist but are illegible. Which prompting pattern best handles this distinction?
explanation: Distinct sentinel values let downstream systems programmatically differentiate between 'not present' and 'illegible' without ambiguity. A single empty string conflates two different conditions. Omitting fields makes it impossible to distinguish missing-from-source vs. extraction-failure. A free-text notes field requires additional NLP to parse and is unreliable for programmatic consumption.
background: When extracting structured data from noisy sources, it's critical to encode the reason for missing data directly in the schema. Using distinct sentinel values (null vs. a specific string) for different absence conditions enables reliable downstream processing without ambiguity or additional inference steps.
options: Use a single empty string for both cases since downstream systems can infer the reason from context
Omit missing fields entirely from the JSON output and include only successfully extracted values
Define separate sentinel values in the schema — use null for fields not present in the document and a specific string like "ILLEGIBLE" for fields that exist but cannot be read
Add a free-text notes field where the model explains which fields had issues and why

text: A team wants certain prompt instructions to apply only when Claude Code is editing files inside the src/api/ directory, but not when working on other parts of the codebase. Which configuration mechanism achieves this?
pattern: Glob-Based Rule Scoping in Claude Code
explanation: The .claude/rules mechanism supports path-scoped loading via glob patterns, activating rules only when matching files are in context. CLAUDE.md does not support runtime conditional blocks. A nested CLAUDE.md adds context but doesn't override or replace the root one. Environment variables don't control Claude Code instruction scoping.
background: Claude Code supports fine-grained rule scoping through .claude/rules files with glob patterns. This allows teams to define directory-specific instructions that activate only when relevant files are being edited, avoiding noise from irrelevant rules in other parts of the codebase.
options: Place a .claude/rules file with a glob pattern matching src/api/** so rules activate only when files in that path are in context
Create a separate CLAUDE.md inside src/api/ which will override the root configuration entirely
Use environment variables in the shell profile to set different Claude Code modes per directory
Add a conditional block in the root CLAUDE.md that checks the current working directory at runtime

text: The November 2025 MCP specification introduced support for Tasks. What is the primary purpose of MCP Tasks?
explanation: MCP Tasks were introduced in the November 2025 spec specifically for async operation tracking with status queries, enabling clients to monitor long-running server-side work. They don't replace tools — tools remain a core primitive. MCP doesn't support peer-to-peer server communication. Tasks are not a batch scheduling system.
background: The November 2025 MCP specification added Tasks as a mechanism for tracking asynchronous operations. When a server performs long-running work (e.g., data processing, file generation), Tasks allow clients to query operation status rather than blocking on synchronous responses.
options: To provide a built-in task queue for scheduling batch processing across multiple clients
To enable tracking of asynchronous operations with queryable status, so clients can monitor long-running server-side work
To replace the tool primitive with a more granular task-based execution model
To allow servers to assign work items to other servers in a peer-to-peer topology

text: You are building an extraction pipeline where Claude must output valid JSON conforming to a strict schema. Which approach provides the strongest guarantee that output will match your schema?
explanation: Tool use with input_schema provides API-level enforcement of JSON schema conformance — the model is constrained to produce valid output matching the schema. System prompt instructions are best-effort and can be violated. Few-shot examples improve compliance but don't guarantee it. Regex post-processing is fragile and can't reliably fix structural JSON issues.
background: Claude's tool_use feature can be leveraged for structured output by defining a tool whose input_schema matches the desired output format. When Claude 'calls' this tool, the output is guaranteed to conform to the schema. This is more reliable than prompt-based approaches because it uses API-level constraints rather than model compliance.
options: Include the JSON schema in the system prompt and ask Claude to follow it carefully
Use a regex-based post-processing step to fix any schema violations in the output
Use the tool_use feature with a JSON Schema defined in the tool's input_schema, which forces Claude to produce conforming structured output
Provide three few-shot examples of correct JSON and rely on pattern matching

text: An MCP server exposes a search_tools capability that supports three detail levels: name-only, name+description, and full schema. What production problem does this tiered approach solve?
explanation: Tiered detail levels enable progressive discovery: agents first browse tool names, narrow down candidates with descriptions, then load full schemas only for tools they'll actually use. This directly reduces token consumption. It's not about access control — all tiers are available to the same agent. It's not about network compression — it's about semantic filtering. Lazy initialization is a server concern unrelated to discovery tiers.
background: Progressive tool discovery organizes tool definitions at multiple detail levels so agents can efficiently narrow their search. In the code-execution-with-MCP pattern, tools are organized as filesystem-based code files with searchable metadata. Agents use name-only listings to scan, descriptions to filter, and full schemas only when ready to invoke — dramatically reducing tokens loaded into context.
options: It allows servers to lazily initialize tools that haven't been fully configured yet
It enables role-based access control by exposing different detail levels to different users
It minimizes token consumption by letting agents discover relevant tools progressively — loading full schemas only for tools they intend to use
It improves network latency by compressing tool definitions into smaller payloads at each tier

text: An agentic system must coordinate three specialized sub-agents (research, coding, review) that each need different tool sets and context. According to MCP architecture principles, which component is responsible for enforcing security policies, handling user authorization, and controlling which servers each client may connect to?
explanation: Correct: The MCP spec explicitly states the host 'controls client connection permissions and lifecycle, enforces security policies and consent requirements, handles user authorization decisions.' Distractor 1 fails because servers operate independently but do NOT self-enforce cross-system security — that's the host's job. Distractor 2 fails because clients are created BY the host and don't independently decide trust. Distractor 3 describes OAuth-style auth which is a transport concern, not the architectural coordination role.
background: In MCP's client-host-server architecture, the host process is the top-level coordinator. It creates and manages multiple client instances, each with a 1:1 relationship to a server. The host enforces all security policies, controls permissions, and handles user consent — servers and clients cannot override these decisions.
options: A shared authorization server that all MCP components query before each operation
Each MCP server, which self-enforces its own security boundaries
The host process, which acts as the container and coordinator for all client instances
The MCP client, which independently decides which servers to trust

text: A developer is building an MCP server that exposes company documentation. The content should be available for the user to browse and for the AI model to read as context, but the model should not be able to modify documents. Which MCP primitive is the best fit?
explanation: Correct: Resources are defined as 'context and data, for the user or the AI model to use' — they are read-only data exposure, perfect for documentation browsing. Distractor 1 fails because Tools are 'functions for the AI model to execute' — they imply action/side-effects and are overkill for passive data. Distractor 2 fails because Prompts are 'templated messages and workflows for users,' not data containers. Distractor 3 fails because Sampling is a client feature allowing servers to request LLM completions, not a data-exposure mechanism.
background: MCP defines three server primitives: Resources (read-only context/data), Tools (executable functions), and Prompts (templated workflows). Choosing the right primitive depends on whether the interaction is passive data exposure, active execution, or user-facing workflow templating. Resources are the correct choice for read-only data the model or user should consume.
options: Resources — they expose context and data for reading by users or the model without side effects
Sampling — it allows the server to request the model to read and summarize documents on demand
Prompts — they provide templated access to data and can be parameterized for document retrieval
Tools — they are the only primitive the model can interact with programmatically

text: An agent connected to 15 MCP servers has all tool definitions loaded upfront into the context window, consuming over 150,000 tokens before the user's first message. According to Anthropic's engineering guidance, what is the recommended architectural change to reduce this overhead?
explanation: Correct: Anthropic's engineering blog explicitly recommends code-execution-with-MCP, where agents write code against MCP server APIs instead of loading all definitions upfront — achieving up to 98.7% token reduction. Distractor 1 fails because max_tokens controls output length, not context capacity, and doesn't reduce the cost of input tokens. Distractor 2 fails because merging tools into one destroys semantic clarity and the model can't distinguish operations. Distractor 3 partially helps with latency on subsequent turns but doesn't address the fundamental problem of 150K tokens occupying the context window on every request.
background: As MCP adoption scales, loading hundreds of tool definitions upfront creates a major token overhead problem. Anthropic's code-execution-with-MCP pattern has agents write code that dynamically discovers and calls tools, loading schemas on-demand. This progressive discovery approach reduced token usage from 150K to ~2K tokens in benchmarks — a 98.7% reduction.
options: Have the agent write code that calls MCP server APIs on demand, loading tool schemas only when needed (code-execution pattern)
Increase the model's max_tokens parameter so the tool definitions occupy a smaller percentage of the window
Concatenate all tool definitions into a single combined tool to reduce per-tool overhead
Cache tool definitions in prompt caching so they are free on subsequent turns

text: During MCP initialization, a server declares it supports tools and resources but not prompts. A client later sends a prompts/list request to this server. What is the expected behavior?
explanation: Correct: MCP's capability negotiation at init means both sides must respect declared capabilities throughout the session. Requesting an undeclared capability is a protocol violation. Distractor 1 sounds reasonable but violates the negotiation contract — an empty list implies the capability exists but has no entries. Distractor 2 fails because capabilities are fixed at init; dynamic upgrade is not part of the protocol. Distractor 3 describes what a well-built client SHOULD do (not send the request), but the question asks about server behavior if it receives one.
background: MCP capability negotiation occurs during initialization: servers declare which primitives they support (resources, tools, prompts) and clients declare theirs (sampling, roots, elicitation). Both sides must respect these declarations throughout the session. Requesting an undeclared capability is a protocol error.
options: The server should return an error because the client is requesting an undeclared capability
The server returns an empty list, since it has no prompts to offer
The server auto-upgrades its capabilities and begins supporting prompts dynamically
The client silently ignores the missing capability and skips the request internally

text: A multi-agent system uses a coordinator that delegates to specialist sub-agents. The coordinator needs to aggregate results from a research agent and a coding agent before presenting a final answer. Which design principle ensures that if one sub-agent fails, the coordinator can still produce a partial result?
explanation: Correct: Composable, independent outputs allow the coordinator to present whatever completed successfully. This follows MCP's design principle that servers (and by extension, agents) provide 'focused functionality in isolation' that can be 'combined seamlessly.' Distractor 1 fails because shared memory creates tight coupling and violates isolation — one agent's failure could corrupt shared state. Distractor 2 fails because sequential chaining means the second agent can't start if the first fails. Distractor 3 fails because cramming everything into one context window defeats the purpose of multi-agent architecture and creates a single point of failure.
background: In multi-agent orchestration, resilient systems treat each sub-agent's output as an independent unit. The coordinator aggregates available results and can present partial answers when some agents fail. This mirrors MCP's composability principle: focused, isolated components that combine seamlessly through a coordinator.
options: Chain the sub-agents sequentially so the second agent can compensate for first-agent failures in its output
Use a shared memory store between sub-agents so each can complete the other's work if one fails
Run all sub-agents in the same context window so the coordinator has full visibility into failure points
Design sub-agent outputs as independent, composable units so the coordinator can degrade gracefully with partial results

text: A developer wants Claude to extract structured data from invoices and return valid JSON. They are using the Messages API. Which approach provides the strongest guarantee that the output will conform to a specific JSON schema?
pattern: Tool Use as Schema Enforcement Mechanism
explanation: Correct: When Claude calls a tool, the input_schema is enforced by the API — the output MUST match the schema. This is the strongest programmatic guarantee. Distractor 1 relies on prompt compliance which can fail with malformed JSON or extra text. Distractor 2 is a fragile post-processing hack that doesn't prevent schema violations. Distractor 3 improves consistency but provides no hard guarantee — the model can still deviate from the examples.
background: Tool use provides the strongest JSON schema enforcement in the Claude API. By defining a tool whose input_schema matches the desired output structure and instructing Claude to 'call' that tool, the API validates the output against the schema. This is more reliable than prompt-based approaches because it's programmatically enforced rather than relying on model compliance.
options: Provide three few-shot examples of correct JSON output in the prompt
Define the target schema as a tool's input_schema and instruct Claude to use that tool, leveraging schema-enforced tool use
Set the system prompt to 'Always respond in valid JSON' and parse the text output
Use a regex post-processor to extract JSON blocks from Claude's free-text response

text: A team has a monorepo with /frontend (React) and /backend (Python) directories. They want Claude Code to use different linting rules depending on which directory the developer is working in. What is the recommended approach?
explanation: Correct: CLAUDE.md files follow a hierarchy — files in subdirectories are loaded when Claude operates in that directory, providing natural directory-scoped configuration. Distractor 1 fails because CLAUDE.md doesn't support conditional logic — it's a static instruction file. Distractor 2 fails because environment variables aren't a Claude Code configuration mechanism for behavioral rules. Distractor 3 is operationally wasteful and doesn't leverage the built-in hierarchy system.
background: Claude Code loads CLAUDE.md files hierarchically: the root file provides baseline instructions, and subdirectory CLAUDE.md files add or override instructions when Claude operates within those directories. This enables monorepos to maintain different coding standards, linting rules, and conventions per project area without conditional logic.
options: Create a single root CLAUDE.md with conditional if/else blocks for each directory
Place separate CLAUDE.md files in /frontend/ and /backend/ directories with directory-specific instructions
Configure two separate Claude Code instances, each locked to one directory
Use environment variables to switch Claude Code's behavior between frontend and backend modes

text: An MCP server needs to support a long-running data export operation that may take several minutes. According to the November 2025 MCP spec, which feature should the server use to let clients track the operation's progress asynchronously?
explanation: Correct: The November 2025 spec explicitly introduced Tasks for 'task-based workflows' enabling async operation tracking with status queries. Distractor 1 fails because Sampling is for server-initiated LLM completions, not polling. Distractor 2 is creative but Resources are for data exposure, not operation lifecycle tracking — and lacks the state machine semantics Tasks provide. Distractor 3 partially works for progress updates but doesn't provide queryable status or completion semantics that Tasks offer.
background: The November 2025 MCP specification release added Tasks as a first-class feature for tracking long-running async operations. Tasks provide a state machine with status queries, allowing clients to check on operation progress without blocking. This was one of the headline additions alongside simplified authorization and elicitation.
options: Notifications — send periodic progress notifications until the operation completes
Resources — expose the export status as a subscribable resource URI
Sampling — request the client to poll the server via LLM-initiated follow-up calls
Tasks — the spec's dedicated mechanism for async operation tracking with status queries

text: An agent queries an MCP server that returns a 50,000-token dataset containing customer records, but the agent only needs records from the last 30 days. Where should the filtering logic execute to minimize token consumption?
explanation: Correct: Anthropic's code-execution-with-MCP pattern specifically recommends filtering data in the execution environment before it enters the context window — this prevents 50K tokens from ever being consumed. Distractor 1 fails because the tokens are already in context even if the model 'ignores' them — they still cost money and occupy space. Distractor 2 fails because PostToolUse can prune results but the timing described ('after the model has already processed it') means tokens were already consumed. Distractor 3 is partially right (server-side filtering is good) but 'exclusively' is wrong — in-environment filtering is the recommended client-side complement.
background: The in-environment data filtering pattern from Anthropic's code-execution-with-MCP approach has agents filter large datasets within the code execution sandbox before results enter the model's context window. This prevents unnecessary token consumption — the model only sees the filtered subset it actually needs for reasoning.
options: In a PostToolUse hook that truncates the tool result after the model has already processed it
On the MCP server side exclusively, since clients should never filter server responses
In the system prompt, instructing the model to ignore records older than 30 days
Inside the code execution environment, filtering data before it enters the model's context window

text: When building an MCP-based system, a developer wants Server A to directly read tool results from Server B to coordinate a workflow. According to MCP design principles, why is this architecture incorrect?
explanation: Correct: MCP explicitly states 'Servers should not be able to read the whole conversation, nor see into other servers' — the host enforces this boundary. Distractor 1 invents a transport-based exception that doesn't exist. Distractor 2 sounds plausible (capability negotiation is real) but server-to-server negotiation isn't part of the protocol. Distractor 3 invents a read/write distinction that doesn't exist in the isolation rules.
background: MCP's architecture enforces strict server isolation. Each server receives only the contextual information it needs, cannot access the full conversation, and cannot see into other servers. All cross-server coordination flows through the host, which acts as the security boundary enforcer and orchestration coordinator.
options: Servers can read from other servers but only if they share the same transport mechanism
Servers are isolated by design — they cannot see into other servers; cross-server interactions must be controlled by the host
Server-to-server communication is allowed but requires mutual capability negotiation first
Direct server communication is permitted for read-only operations but blocked for write operations

text: A prompt asks Claude to categorize customer feedback into predefined categories. During testing, Claude occasionally returns a category not in the predefined list when the feedback is ambiguous. What is the recommended prompt engineering fix?
explanation: Correct: Providing an explicit escape hatch ('Other/Uncategorizable') prevents Claude from inventing categories while still handling ambiguous inputs faithfully. This follows the pattern of making all valid states explicit. Distractor 1 fails because higher temperature increases randomness, making hallucinated categories MORE likely. Distractor 2 is a fragile workaround that doesn't fix the root cause and can silently miscategorize. Distractor 3 is impractical — you can't pre-filter ambiguous inputs in production.
background: When building classification systems with Claude, the predefined category set must account for ambiguous or edge-case inputs. Adding an explicit fallback category gives the model a valid output path for uncertain cases, preventing it from inventing new categories. This is analogous to the 'explicit unknown enum' pattern for structured extraction.
options: Add an explicit 'Other/Uncategorizable' category to the list and instruct Claude to use it when no predefined category fits
Remove ambiguous feedback from the input dataset before sending to Claude
Add a post-processing step that maps unknown categories to the closest predefined one via string matching
Raise the temperature to give Claude more flexibility in choosing from the predefined categories

text: An MCP server exposes a search_tools function that accepts a detail_level parameter with values name-only, name+description, and full-schema. What production problem does this progressive disclosure pattern solve?
explanation: Correct: Progressive disclosure is explicitly described in Anthropic's code-execution-with-MCP pattern — agents first browse tool names, then descriptions, then load full schemas only for tools they'll actually use. This directly reduces token consumption. Distractor 1 confuses discovery granularity with access control — all detail levels are available to authorized agents. Distractor 2 is a secondary benefit but not the architectural motivation. Distractor 3 invents a versioning use case not associated with this pattern.
background: Progressive tool discovery organizes tool information into tiers of detail. Agents first discover tool names (low token cost), then selectively load descriptions, and finally retrieve full schemas only for tools they need. This pattern is central to the code-execution-with-MCP approach, preventing thousands of tokens from being wasted on tools the agent never calls.
options: It speeds up MCP server response times by returning less data per request
It enforces access control by hiding sensitive tool parameters from unauthorized agents
It reduces context window consumption by letting agents load only the level of tool detail they need at each stage
It enables backward compatibility when tool schemas change between server versions

text: A CI/CD pipeline uses Claude Code in headless mode to review pull requests. The pipeline needs to ensure Claude cannot execute arbitrary shell commands during the review. What is the correct configuration approach?
explanation: Correct: The --allowedTools flag programmatically restricts which tools Claude can invoke, providing a hard enforcement boundary. Distractor 1 relies on prompt compliance which is not a security boundary — this is the classic programmatic enforcement vs. prompt guidance distinction. Distractor 2 is wrong because plan mode is for generating plans, not for restricting tool access, and may still allow execution. Distractor 3 is an OS-level workaround that doesn't integrate with Claude's tool system and can break legitimate operations.
background: When running Claude Code in CI/CD headless mode, security requires programmatic restrictions on available tools rather than prompt-based instructions. The --allowedTools flag provides a whitelist of permitted tools, ensuring Claude cannot access shell, file system, or other capabilities beyond what the pipeline specifically allows.
options: Configure the CI runner's OS permissions to block shell access for the Claude process
Set a system prompt telling Claude 'Do not run shell commands' and rely on instruction following
Use the --allowedTools flag to explicitly whitelist only the tools Claude may use during the CI run
Run Claude Code in plan mode, which automatically disables all tool execution

text: An MCP client is about to forward tool result data to the language model for processing. The data contains customer PII (emails, phone numbers). According to MCP security principles, what should the client do?
explanation: Correct: MCP security guidance states that PII can be intercepted and tokenized by the MCP client before model exposure — the client is the appropriate layer for this sanitization. Distractor 1 fails because the server returning data doesn't mean it's safe for model consumption — the client has independent security responsibilities. Distractor 2 fails because sending PII to the model for it to redact defeats the purpose — the model has already 'seen' the PII. Distractor 3 is overly aggressive — the data is legitimate, it just needs sanitization.
background: In MCP architecture, the client sits between the server and the model, making it the ideal layer for data sanitization. MCP security principles specify that PII can be intercepted and tokenized by the client before the data reaches the model. This ensures sensitive information never enters the model's context window while still allowing the workflow to proceed.
options: Intercept and tokenize or redact PII before it enters the model's context, since the client sits between server and model
Ask the model to identify and redact PII from the data as its first processing step
Reject the tool result entirely and return an error to the user
Pass the data through unchanged since the MCP server already validated it was safe to return

text: An MCP server needs to collect additional configuration from the user during a workflow — for example, asking which output format they prefer before generating a report. Which MCP feature, introduced in the November 2025 spec, enables this server-initiated user interaction?
explanation: Correct: Elicitation is explicitly defined as 'server-initiated requests for additional information from users' — introduced in the November 2025 spec for exactly this use case. Distractor 1 is close but Prompts are pre-defined templates selected by users upfront, not mid-workflow server-initiated requests. Distractor 2 fails because Sampling requests LLM completions, not direct user input. Distractor 3 fails because Roots provide filesystem/URI boundary information, not arbitrary user preferences.
background: Elicitation is a client feature introduced in the November 2025 MCP spec that enables servers to request additional information from users through secure, out-of-band interactions. Unlike Prompts (which are pre-defined templates), Elicitation allows servers to dynamically ask for user input during a workflow — such as configuration preferences, disambiguation choices, or consent confirmations.
options: Sampling — the server requests an LLM completion that includes a question for the user
Elicitation — a client feature that allows servers to request additional information from users through the client
Prompts — the server exposes a parameterized prompt template that the user fills in before execution
Roots — the server queries the client for the user's configured workspace boundaries

text: According to MCP's design principles, why are servers intentionally prevented from reading the full conversation history?
explanation: The MCP spec explicitly states that servers should not be able to read the whole conversation or see into other servers — this is a security and isolation principle, not a technical limitation of JSON-RPC. The host enforces these boundaries. Token reduction is a side benefit, not the design motivation. JSON-RPC can pass arbitrary data structures. Servers don't maintain independent user conversations.
background: MCP's architecture isolates servers by design. Each server receives only the contextual information it needs to fulfill its role. The host process acts as the security coordinator, controlling what information flows to each server and preventing servers from accessing each other's data or the full conversation.
options: To allow servers to maintain their own independent conversation threads with users
To reduce token usage by keeping conversation history out of server memory
Because JSON-RPC 2.0 does not support passing conversation arrays between processes
To enforce isolation — servers receive only the contextual information necessary for their function, with the host controlling cross-server boundaries

text: You need Claude to extract a list of mentioned companies from earnings call transcripts. Some transcripts mention zero companies. Which prompt strategy most reliably handles this edge case?
explanation: Returning an empty array keeps the output schema consistent — downstream code always processes an array without special-case string parsing. Returning 'NONE' breaks type consistency and requires extra handling. Omitting instructions leads to unpredictable outputs (the model might narrate 'no companies found' instead). A boolean guard adds unnecessary complexity and risks inconsistency between the flag and the array.
background: When designing extraction prompts with structured output, the schema should handle edge cases natively. Empty arrays are the idiomatic way to represent 'zero items found' in JSON, keeping the type contract stable and eliminating the need for sentinel values or conditional parsing logic.
options: Instruct the model to return the string "NONE" when no companies are mentioned
Omit any instruction about the empty case and let the model infer the correct behavior
Add a boolean field has_companies and only parse the array when it is true
Define the output schema with an array field and instruct the model to return an empty array [] when no companies are found

text: An agentic loop has been running for 45 turns and the context window is 85% full. The agent still needs to complete several more tool calls. What is the recommended approach?
explanation: Context compaction (summarizing older turns) preserves continuity while freeing space for new interactions. Re-executing all tool calls from scratch is wasteful and may produce different results. max_tokens controls output length, not context window size. Removing the system prompt eliminates critical instructions and behavioral guardrails.
background: Long-running agentic loops can exhaust the context window. The compaction pattern summarizes earlier conversation turns into a condensed representation while keeping the system prompt and recent messages intact. This lets the agent continue its task without losing critical context or restarting from zero.
options: Remove the system prompt to free up space for additional tool call results
Start a completely new conversation and re-execute all previous tool calls from scratch
Use context compaction to summarize earlier turns while preserving the system prompt and recent messages, then continue the loop
Increase max_tokens to expand the context window beyond its limit

text: An MCP server exposes a search_documents tool that returns large result sets. According to the code-execution-with-MCP pattern, how should the agent reduce token consumption when processing these results?
explanation: The code-execution-with-MCP pattern has agents write code that calls tools and filters/aggregates data in the execution environment, so only the relevant subset enters the context window — achieving up to 98.7% token reduction. A global max_tokens on the server would blindly truncate results. Extended thinking still requires the full results in context first. Splitting queries and concatenating raw results still bloats context.
background: When agents interact with data-heavy MCP tools, direct tool calls pass all results through the context window. By writing code that calls tools and processes results in-environment, agents filter data before it enters context, dramatically reducing token consumption and improving latency.
options: Split the search into multiple smaller queries and concatenate the raw results in context
Have the model request all results and use extended thinking to summarize them internally
Write code in the execution environment to call the tool and filter or aggregate results before returning only the relevant subset to the model's context
Set a global max_tokens parameter on the MCP server to truncate all tool responses

text: A monorepo has CLAUDE.md files at the root, in packages/api/, and in packages/web/. A developer runs Claude Code while their working directory is packages/api/. Which CLAUDE.md files are loaded?
explanation: Claude Code loads CLAUDE.md files from the current working directory and all ancestor directories up to the project root. packages/web/CLAUDE.md is a sibling directory, not an ancestor, so it is not loaded. Child files do not override parent files — they layer on top. Loading all three regardless of directory would violate scoping. Subdirectory files don't require explicit imports — they're loaded automatically when in scope.
background: CLAUDE.md configuration follows a hierarchical loading model. When Claude Code starts, it walks up the directory tree from the current working directory, loading every CLAUDE.md it finds. This creates layered configuration where project-wide settings live at the root and directory-specific overrides live closer to the code.
options: Both the root CLAUDE.md and the packages/api/CLAUDE.md are loaded; packages/web/CLAUDE.md is not
Only packages/api/CLAUDE.md is loaded — child files override parent files entirely
All three CLAUDE.md files are loaded regardless of working directory
Only the root CLAUDE.md is loaded — subdirectory files require explicit import

text: In a multi-agent system, Agent A delegates a subtask to Agent B, which encounters a tool error it cannot resolve. Following the structured error propagation principle, what should Agent B do?
explanation: Structured error propagation requires the subagent to return a clear, structured description of the failure to its coordinator so the coordinator can decide next steps (retry, reassign, or escalate). Infinite retries waste resources and may never succeed. Bypassing the coordinator breaks the orchestration hierarchy. Returning silent partial results makes failures invisible and leads to incorrect downstream decisions.
background: In multi-agent architectures, subagents must communicate failures back to their coordinator in a structured format. This enables the coordinator to make informed decisions about retries, alternative strategies, or escalation — rather than having subagents independently decide how to handle errors they were not designed to resolve.
options: Return a structured error result to Agent A describing what failed, what was attempted, and why it could not be resolved
Retry the tool call indefinitely until it succeeds or the context window fills up
Escalate directly to the human user, bypassing Agent A
Silently return a partial result and let Agent A infer that something went wrong

text: You want Claude to output valid JSON matching a specific schema. Which approach provides the strongest guarantee that the output conforms to the schema?
explanation: When a tool's input_schema is defined, Claude's API enforces that the tool call arguments match the schema — providing the strongest structural guarantee. System prompt instructions can be ignored or misinterpreted. Few-shot examples improve likelihood but don't guarantee conformance. Post-processing can catch errors but can't always fix them correctly, and it adds latency.
background: Tool use provides a mechanism for schema-enforced structured output. By defining the desired output structure as a tool's input_schema, developers leverage the API's built-in validation rather than relying on prompt-based instructions, which are probabilistic rather than deterministic.
options: Provide three few-shot examples of correct JSON output in the prompt
Include the JSON schema in the system prompt and ask Claude to follow it carefully
Use a regex-based post-processing step to validate and fix the JSON after generation
Define the schema as a tool's input_schema and have Claude 'call' the tool — the API enforces the schema on the output

text: An MCP client and server perform capability negotiation during initialization. The server declares it supports tools and resources but not prompts. What happens if the client later sends a prompts/list request?
explanation: MCP's capability negotiation is binding — both parties must respect declared capabilities throughout the session. If the server didn't declare prompts support, a prompts/list request is invalid and should be rejected. Returning an empty list would imply the capability exists but has no entries. Clients don't silently swap request types. The three primitives are optional, not mandatory.
background: During MCP session initialization, clients and servers exchange capability declarations. These declarations define the contract for the session — neither party should send requests for undeclared capabilities. This prevents runtime errors and ensures both sides have clear expectations about available functionality.
options: The client automatically falls back to listing tools instead of prompts
The server should reject the request because it did not declare prompt capability during negotiation
The server must return an empty list to indicate no prompts are available
The request succeeds because all three primitives are always available regardless of negotiation

text: A developer builds a customer support agent that handles 50+ turn conversations. After about 40 turns, the agent starts contradicting instructions from its system prompt. What is the most likely cause?
explanation: In long conversations, the system prompt's influence can weaken as it becomes proportionally smaller relative to the growing conversation. The model hasn't 'forgotten' it — the prompt is still in context, but attention is distributed across more content. There is no fixed turn limit that invalidates system prompts. Tool results don't corrupt prompt encoding. The solution is context compaction or periodic re-anchoring of key instructions.
background: As conversations grow, the system prompt occupies a smaller fraction of the context window. While it remains present, the model's attention mechanism may give it less weight relative to recent messages. This is why long-running agents benefit from context compaction and periodic reinforcement of critical instructions in assistant-turn prefills or mid-conversation reminders.
options: The model has permanently forgotten the system prompt after processing too many user messages
The system prompt has been pushed far from the model's attention window by accumulated conversation turns, weakening its influence
The system prompt was invalidated when the conversation exceeded a fixed 20-turn limit
Tool results in the conversation corrupted the system prompt's encoding

text: A team wants Claude Code to automatically run npm test after every file write during an interactive session. Which mechanism should they use?
explanation: PostToolUse hooks are the correct mechanism — they fire automatically after a specified tool executes, enabling reliable post-write validation. CLAUDE.md instructions are advisory and Claude may not always follow them. A cron job is unrelated to Claude Code's lifecycle and would run unnecessarily. A PreToolUse hook on Read fires before reading, not after writing — wrong tool and wrong timing.
background: Claude Code supports lifecycle hooks (PreToolUse and PostToolUse) that run shell commands automatically when specific tools are invoked. PostToolUse hooks on the Write tool are commonly used to run linters, formatters, or test suites immediately after code changes, ensuring continuous validation during development sessions.
options: Create a cron job on the developer's machine to run npm test every 30 seconds
Use a PreToolUse hook on the Read tool to run tests before any file is opened
Add 'Always run npm test after writing files' to the CLAUDE.md system instructions
Configure a PostToolUse hook that triggers on the Write tool and executes npm test

text: You are designing an agent that must book flights, reserve hotels, and arrange car rentals. Each service has its own API and failure modes. Which architecture best isolates failures and simplifies each agent's responsibilities?
explanation: A coordinator with specialized subagents follows MCP's design principle that servers (and by extension, agents) should focus on specific, well-defined capabilities. This isolates failures — a hotel API outage doesn't crash the flight agent. A monolithic agent creates a single point of failure with complex error handling. Direct peer-to-peer communication creates tight coupling. A strict pipeline doesn't work because these bookings are often parallel, not sequential.
background: Multi-agent architectures benefit from decomposing complex tasks into domain-specific subagents coordinated by a central orchestrator. Each subagent encapsulates one domain's tools, error handling, and retry logic. The coordinator manages the overall workflow, aggregates results, and handles cross-domain dependencies.
options: A coordinator agent that delegates to three specialized subagents — one for flights, one for hotels, and one for car rentals
Three independent agents that communicate directly with each other via shared state
A pipeline architecture where the flight agent's output feeds into the hotel agent, which feeds into the car agent
A single monolithic agent with all three APIs exposed as tools, handling all logic in one loop

text: An MCP server needs to provide a read-only company directory that an LLM can reference during conversations. Which MCP primitive is most appropriate?
explanation: Resources are MCP's primitive for exposing read-only context and data — exactly matching a company directory use case. A tool would work but is semantically wrong (tools are for actions/computations, not static data). Prompts are for templated workflows, not data exposure. Sampling is for server-initiated LLM interactions, not data serving.
background: MCP distinguishes between Resources (read-only data/context), Tools (executable functions the model can invoke), and Prompts (templated message workflows). Choosing the right primitive ensures semantic clarity and allows clients to handle each type appropriately — for example, resources can be cached and subscribed to for updates.
options: Tools — define a get_directory tool that returns the full directory on each call
Prompts — create a templated prompt that embeds the directory data
Sampling — use server-initiated sampling to inject directory data into the conversation
Resources — they are designed for exposing read-only contextual data to the model

text: You ask Claude to classify customer emails into categories using a JSON schema with an enum of five values. During testing, you find that 8% of emails don't fit any category and Claude forces them into the closest match. What should you change?
pattern: Enum Escape Hatch for Edge Cases
explanation: Adding an explicit 'other' category gives the model a valid output for genuinely ambiguous inputs, preventing forced misclassification. Removing the enum entirely sacrifices schema consistency and makes downstream processing unreliable. Temperature 0 makes output more deterministic but doesn't solve the missing-category problem. More few-shot examples can't cover every possible email type and don't address the fundamental schema gap.
background: When using enum-constrained output schemas, it's important to include a catch-all category for inputs that don't fit predefined values. Without one, the model is forced to misclassify ambiguous cases. The catch-all should be explicitly named (e.g., 'other' or 'uncategorized') rather than relying on the model to handle edge cases creatively.
options: Remove the enum constraint entirely and let the model return free-text categories
Add an "other" value to the enum so the model has an explicit escape hatch for ambiguous inputs
Add a temperature of 0 to ensure more deterministic classification
Increase the number of few-shot examples to cover every possible email type

text: An agentic system uses Claude to coordinate three specialist subagents. When one subagent encounters an unrecoverable error, how should the coordinator handle it to maintain overall system reliability?
explanation: Correct: Coordinating partial completion preserves value from successful subtasks while transparently reporting failures. Distractor 1 fails because aborting all work wastes successful results. Distractor 2 risks infinite loops and context exhaustion. Distractor 3 is dangerous because silently hiding failures misleads the user about result completeness.
background: In production multi-agent systems, individual subtask failures are expected. A resilient coordinator should isolate failures, preserve successful results, and clearly communicate which parts succeeded and which failed. This prevents cascading failures while maintaining user trust.

refs.label: Claude Agent SDK — How the agent loop works

options: The coordinator silently ignores the failure and presents the partial result as if it were complete
The coordinator immediately terminates all subagents and returns the raw error to the user
The coordinator catches the error, marks that subtask as failed, and continues with the remaining subagents before presenting a partial result to the user
The failed subagent should retry indefinitely until it succeeds or the context window is exhausted

text: According to the MCP specification, which transport mechanism is appropriate for connecting to an MCP server running as a local subprocess on the same machine?
pattern: Stdio Transport for Local MCP Servers
explanation: Correct: The MCP spec defines stdio as the transport for local process communication, where the host spawns the server and communicates via stdin/stdout. Distractor 1 is wrong because Streamable HTTP is designed for remote services, not local subprocesses. Distractor 2 is wrong because WebSocket is not a defined MCP transport. Distractor 3 is wrong because MCP uses JSON-RPC 2.0 over defined transports, not custom binary protocols.
background: MCP defines two transport mechanisms: stdio for local processes (host spawns server as a subprocess) and Streamable HTTP for remote services. The stdio transport is simpler and avoids network overhead for same-machine communication. Both transports carry JSON-RPC 2.0 messages.
options: Unix domain sockets with a custom binary protocol
stdio transport, communicating via standard input and standard output
WebSocket transport with TLS encryption for local IPC
Streamable HTTP transport over localhost:8080 with OAuth authorization

text: You need Claude to extract structured data from invoices. The output must strictly conform to a JSON schema with specific field names and types. Which approach provides the strongest schema enforcement guarantee?
pattern: Tool Input Schema for Output Validation
explanation: Correct: When Claude calls a tool, the API validates the arguments against the tool's input_schema, providing API-level enforcement. Distractor 1 relies on prompt compliance with no runtime validation. Distractor 2 improves consistency but doesn't guarantee schema conformance. Distractor 3 is fragile and can fail on unexpected formatting.
background: Tool use provides the strongest schema enforcement because the API itself validates tool call arguments against the declared JSON Schema. This shifts validation from prompt-level guidance (which can fail) to API-level enforcement. The pattern treats a 'dummy' tool as an output schema contract.
options: Include the JSON schema in the system prompt and ask Claude to output valid JSON
Use a regex-based post-processing step to extract JSON from Claude's free-text response
Provide three few-shot examples of correct JSON output in the prompt
Define the output schema as a tool's input_schema and instruct Claude to call that tool with the extracted data

text: A long-running agent session has consumed 85% of its context window. The architect wants to continue the session without losing critical state. Which technique should be applied?
explanation: Correct: Summarization compresses earlier turns while preserving essential state, allowing the session to continue. Distractor 1 is wrong because max_tokens controls output length, not the context window size. Distractor 2 is impractical mid-session as it loses session state and requires re-establishing context. Distractor 3 risks losing critical information that affects subsequent reasoning.
background: When an agent session approaches context limits, conversation summarization is the recommended technique. The agent (or orchestrator) generates a concise summary of prior interactions and replaces verbose history with it. This preserves decision-relevant state while freeing tokens for continued work.
options: Silently drop the oldest messages from the conversation without summarization
Switch to a different model mid-session that has a larger context window
Increase the max_tokens parameter to expand the available context window
Summarize the conversation history so far and replace earlier messages with the summary, freeing context for new interactions

text: An MCP server exposes a prompts primitive alongside tools. What is the intended purpose of MCP prompts compared to tools?
pattern: MCP Prompts vs Tools Intent Distinction
explanation: Correct: Per the MCP spec, prompts are user-facing templated workflows while tools are model-facing executable functions — they serve different interaction patterns. Distractor 1 is wrong because prompts don't override system prompts. Distractor 2 confuses prompts with caching mechanisms. Distractor 3 is wrong because the spec explicitly distinguishes their purposes and invocation patterns.
background: MCP defines three server primitives: Resources (data/context), Tools (model-executable functions), and Prompts (templated messages/workflows). Prompts are designed for user-driven selection — they represent pre-built interaction templates. Tools are designed for AI-model-driven invocation during autonomous operation.
options: Prompts are cached versions of tool definitions used to reduce token consumption in long sessions
Prompts and tools are interchangeable primitives; the distinction is purely cosmetic
Prompts define system-level instructions that override the host's system prompt, while tools are user-triggered actions
Prompts are templated messages and workflows designed for users to select and invoke, while tools are functions designed for the AI model to call autonomously

text: A team wants to enforce a specific code style rule only when Claude Code edits files in the src/api/ directory. Where should this rule be placed?
explanation: Correct: CLAUDE.md files placed in a subdirectory are loaded only when Claude Code operates in that directory or its descendants, providing automatic path-based scoping. Distractor 1 is wrong because comments in the root file have no enforcement mechanism — Claude may apply the rule everywhere. Distractor 2 is wrong because user-level CLAUDE.md applies globally. Distractor 3 is wrong because settings.json controls tool permissions and hooks, not prompt-level style rules.
background: Claude Code loads CLAUDE.md files from the ancestor chain of the current working directory. Placing a CLAUDE.md in a specific subdirectory ensures its instructions apply only when Claude operates on files in that subtree. This enables directory-scoped configuration without polluting the root-level instructions.
options: In a .claude/settings.json file with a glob pattern targeting src/api/**
In a src/api/CLAUDE.md file, which is loaded only when Claude operates on files in that directory or its children
In the project root CLAUDE.md with a comment indicating it applies only to src/api/
In ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md with a conditional path check in the instruction text

text: When building an agent loop that calls Claude's Messages API, the agent should continue looping when the response's stop_reason is tool_use. Which stop_reason value signals that the model has finished its turn and the agent loop should exit?
explanation: Correct: The Messages API returns stop_reason 'end_turn' when the model naturally finishes its response without requesting tool use. Distractor 1 is wrong because 'complete' is not a valid stop_reason value. Distractor 2 is wrong because 'done' is not a valid stop_reason value. Distractor 3 is wrong because stop_sequence is for custom stop strings, not the standard agent loop termination signal.
background: The Claude Messages API returns several stop_reason values: 'end_turn' (model finished naturally), 'tool_use' (model wants to call a tool), 'max_tokens' (output limit reached), and 'stop_sequence' (custom stop string matched). An agent loop should continue on 'tool_use' and typically exit on 'end_turn'.
options: complete
done
end_turn
stop_sequence is the only way to terminate an agent loop

text: An MCP host application connects to five MCP servers simultaneously. According to the MCP architecture, how many MCP client instances does the host create?
pattern: Host Creates One Client Per Server
explanation: Correct: Per the MCP architecture spec, each client has a 1:1 relationship with a server, so five servers require five client instances. Distractor 1 is wrong because MCP explicitly requires isolated 1:1 client-server sessions. Distractor 2 invents a redundancy pattern not in the spec. Distractor 3 is wrong because the 1:1 relationship applies regardless of transport type.
background: The MCP architecture specifies that a host creates and manages multiple clients, with each client maintaining a 1:1 stateful session with exactly one server. This isolation is a deliberate design choice ensuring that servers cannot see into each other's sessions and security boundaries remain clear.
options: It depends on the transport; stdio servers share one client while HTTP servers each get their own
Ten — two clients per server for redundancy and failover
One — a single client multiplexes connections to all five servers
Five — one client instance per server, each maintaining an independent session

text: An architect is building a pipeline that processes 500 PDF documents through Claude for data extraction. Each document requires a separate API call. Which API feature should they use to minimize cost and maximize throughput?
explanation: Correct: The Message Batches API is designed for high-volume async workloads, offering 50% cost savings and higher throughput limits. Distractor 1 is wrong because prompt caching reduces costs for repeated prefixes in individual calls, not for batch processing of distinct documents. Distractor 2 is wrong because streaming is per-request and doesn't batch multiple documents. Distractor 3 invents a non-existent batch upload parameter.
background: The Message Batches API allows sending up to thousands of requests as a batch for asynchronous processing. Results are available within 24 hours and cost 50% less than synchronous API calls. This is ideal for data extraction pipelines, evaluations, and other workloads that don't require real-time responses.
options: Prompt caching, which stores all 500 documents in a persistent server-side cache for reuse
The Files API with a batch parameter that uploads all documents in one request
Streaming mode, which processes all documents in a single long-lived connection
The Message Batches API, which processes requests asynchronously at a 50% cost reduction

text: A developer wants Claude Code to automatically run npm test after every file write to catch regressions immediately. Which configuration mechanism should they use?
explanation: Correct: PostToolUse hooks in settings.json execute programmatically after a specified tool completes, providing guaranteed execution. Distractor 1 relies on prompt compliance which Claude may skip under certain conditions. Distractor 2 runs tests before the write happens, which wouldn't catch regressions from the current change. Distractor 3 is over-engineered — hooks are the native mechanism for this.
background: Claude Code supports lifecycle hooks (PreToolUse and PostToolUse) configured in .claude/settings.json. These hooks run shell commands before or after specific tool invocations. PostToolUse on the Write tool is a common pattern for running linters, formatters, or test suites automatically after code changes.
options: A custom MCP server that monitors the filesystem and runs tests on file changes
A PostToolUse hook in .claude/settings.json that triggers on the Write tool
A PreToolUse hook that executes the test suite before allowing the write to proceed
An instruction in CLAUDE.md that says 'Always run npm test after writing files'

text: According to MCP's design principles, what level of complexity should an individual MCP server aim for?
explanation: Correct: The MCP spec explicitly states that servers should be extremely easy to build, focus on specific well-defined capabilities, and have simple interfaces. Distractor 1 contradicts the simplicity principle — full API surfaces create bloated servers. Distractor 2 contradicts the design where multi-step coordination belongs in the host. Distractor 3 has no basis in the spec.
background: MCP's design principles emphasize that servers should be extremely easy to build, focusing on specific well-defined capabilities. Complex orchestration is the host's responsibility. Simple, focused servers are highly composable — multiple servers can be combined seamlessly, and modular design supports extensibility.
options: Each server should be comprehensive, exposing the full API surface of the underlying system it wraps
Server complexity should match the host application's complexity to ensure balanced architecture
Servers should be complex enough to handle multi-step workflows internally without returning to the client
Each server should be simple, focusing on specific well-defined capabilities with minimal implementation overhead

text: An agent receives a stop_reason of max_tokens during a multi-step task. What does this indicate and how should the agent respond?
explanation: Correct: stop_reason 'max_tokens' means the output was cut short by the max_tokens parameter, not that the context is full. The agent can send the truncated response back to continue. Distractor 1 confuses output limits with context window limits. Distractor 2 is the opposite — max_tokens means the response is incomplete. Distractor 3 confuses token limits with billing limits.
background: When Claude's response hits the max_tokens output limit, the API returns stop_reason 'max_tokens'. This is distinct from end_turn (natural completion) and tool_use (tool call requested). Agents should handle this by continuing the conversation, allowing the model to complete its interrupted output.
options: The context window is full and no more messages can be added; the agent must start a new session
The model has completed its response and is signaling that no more tokens are needed
A billing limit was reached and the API will reject further requests until the next billing cycle
The model's response was truncated because it hit the output token limit; the agent should continue the conversation to let the model finish its response

text: When designing a classification prompt, a developer wants Claude to output a confidence score alongside each classification. Which approach best ensures the confidence score is calibrated and useful?
explanation: Correct: Defining explicit criteria for confidence levels grounds the score in observable features, producing more consistent and interpretable results. Distractor 1 is wrong because LLM-generated probabilities are not inherently well-calibrated. Distractor 2 is wrong because determinism doesn't equal calibration — the score may be consistently wrong. Distractor 3 is wrong because Claude's API does not expose logprobs for this purpose.
background: When asking Claude for confidence assessments, vague requests like 'rate your confidence from 0-1' produce poorly calibrated scores. Instead, define concrete criteria for each confidence tier (high/medium/low) based on observable features in the input. This produces more consistent, interpretable, and actionable confidence signals.
options: Ask Claude to output a probability between 0 and 1, as LLMs natively produce well-calibrated probabilities
Define specific criteria for each confidence level (e.g., 'high' means all key indicators present, 'low' means ambiguous signals) rather than asking for a raw probability
Use temperature 0 to ensure confidence scores are always deterministic and therefore reliable
Request logprobs for the classification token and use that as the confidence score

text: An architect needs Claude to process a user query that references data from three different MCP servers. According to MCP architecture, which component is responsible for aggregating context from these servers before presenting it to the model?
explanation: Correct: Per the MCP spec, the host controls AI/LLM integration and manages context aggregation across clients. Distractor 1 is wrong because servers are isolated by design and cannot communicate with each other. Distractor 2 is wrong because each client has a 1:1 relationship with one server — there's no broadcast mechanism. Distractor 3 is wrong because the model doesn't directly contact servers; the host mediates all interactions.
background: In MCP's architecture, the host is the central coordinator. It creates client instances (one per server), manages their lifecycles, aggregates context from multiple servers, and presents unified context to the AI model. Servers are intentionally isolated from each other, and the host enforces all security boundaries.
options: The MCP client broadcasts the query to all servers simultaneously and merges their responses automatically
The model itself requests context from each server directly during inference
The servers coordinate among themselves using cross-server messaging to assemble the full context
The host application, which manages all client connections and coordinates context aggregation across servers

text: An agentic application uses Claude to process multi-step tasks. After each API response, the orchestrator checks stop_reason. When the model returns stop_reason: "max_tokens", what is the correct production-safe behaviour?
explanation: Correct: max_tokens means the model's output was cut short — the orchestrator must detect this and decide whether to continue or alert the user. Distractor 1 fails because treating truncated output as complete leads to data loss or malformed downstream results. Distractor 2 fails because max_tokens is not a transient error — retrying with the same parameters produces the same truncation. Distractor 3 fails because max_tokens is unrelated to safety refusals (those surface as stop_reason: end_turn with refusal content).
background: The Messages API returns stop_reason to indicate why generation stopped. 'max_tokens' means the output hit the configured limit before the model finished. Production agents must handle this distinctly from 'end_turn' (natural completion) and 'tool_use' (tool call pending). Ignoring truncation is a common source of silent data corruption in agentic pipelines.
options: Detect truncation, then either extend the response with a continuation request or surface the incomplete output to the user for decision
Immediately retry the same request with identical parameters, since max_tokens indicates a transient error
Treat the response as complete and pass it to the next pipeline stage
Discard the response and escalate to a human operator, since max_tokens always indicates a safety refusal

text: When designing MCP servers for a multi-agent system, you want to ensure that Server A cannot observe the tools, resources, or conversation context available through Server B. Which MCP architectural principle guarantees this?
explanation: Correct: MCP's architecture mandates that servers cannot see into other servers — the host mediates all cross-server interactions. Distractor 1 is wrong because there is no mutual visibility negotiation between servers; they are isolated by design. Distractor 2 is wrong because servers do NOT share a common namespace — isolation is the default. Distractor 3 is wrong because isolation is not opt-in; it is an architectural invariant enforced by the host.
background: MCP's client-host-server architecture enforces that each server connection is isolated. Servers receive only the contextual information necessary for their operation. The full conversation history stays with the host, and cross-server interactions are controlled exclusively by the host process. This is a foundational security principle, not an optional configuration.
options: Servers opt into isolation by setting an 'isolated: true' flag in their capability declaration
The MCP specification requires all servers to share a common resource namespace so the host can deduplicate
Each client maintains an isolated session with its server, and the host enforces security boundaries that prevent cross-server visibility
Servers negotiate mutual visibility during the capability exchange phase of initialization

text: You need Claude to extract structured data from legal documents. The schema has 15 fields, but only 3 are relevant to any single document type. When configuring the tool schema, what is the recommended approach for fields that are genuinely not applicable?
explanation: Correct: Making all fields required with explicit null for N/A ensures consistent schema shape, making downstream parsing reliable. Distractor 1 fails because optional fields lead to ambiguity — you cannot distinguish 'model forgot the field' from 'field was not applicable'. Distractor 2 fails because it creates schema proliferation and requires pre-classifying the document type before extraction. Distractor 3 fails because free-text output defeats the purpose of structured extraction.
background: In structured extraction, a consistent output shape is critical for downstream processing. The recommended pattern is to define all fields as required and use null to explicitly signal that a field is not applicable. This eliminates ambiguity between 'the model missed this field' and 'this field does not apply,' which is impossible to distinguish when fields are optional.
options: Mark all 15 fields as required in the schema and instruct the model to return null for fields that do not apply to the document type
Use a single required free-text field where the model describes which of the 15 fields were found
Create 5 separate tool schemas, one per document type, each containing only the relevant subset of fields
Make the 12 conditionally-irrelevant fields optional so the model can omit them entirely from the output

text: During a long multi-turn agentic session, Claude's responses begin to ignore instructions placed in the system prompt. What is the most likely cause and the recommended mitigation?
pattern: System Prompt Re-Injection for Long Sessions
explanation: Correct: In very long contexts, earlier content (including system prompts) receives proportionally less attention, causing instruction drift. Re-injecting or compacting fixes this. Distractor 1 is wrong because the API never removes the system prompt. Distractor 2 is wrong because there is no deliberate deprioritisation mechanism — it is an emergent attention phenomenon. Distractor 3 is wrong because system prompt tokens are part of the input context window, not subtracted from max_tokens.
background: System prompt attention decay is a well-known phenomenon in long-context LLM interactions. As the conversation grows, the relative attention weight given to early context (including the system prompt) diminishes. Production mitigations include periodically re-injecting critical instructions into recent turns, using context compaction to summarize older turns while preserving system instructions, or structuring prompts to place critical constraints near the end of context.
options: Attention decay over long contexts causes the system prompt to lose influence; mitigate by re-injecting key instructions in recent user turns or using context compaction
The system prompt token count is subtracted from max_tokens, leaving too few tokens for the response
The system prompt is automatically removed after 50 turns by the API to save tokens
Claude deliberately deprioritises system prompts in favour of the most recent user message as a safety measure

text: An MCP server exposes a prompts capability. According to the MCP specification, what is the primary intent of MCP Prompts compared to MCP Tools?
explanation: Correct: The MCP spec defines Prompts as templated messages and workflows for users, whereas Tools are functions for the AI model to execute. Distractor 1 inverts the relationship — prompts are not auto-injected system instructions. Distractor 2 is wrong because prompts do not execute code; they provide message templates. Distractor 3 is wrong because the distinction is semantic and affects how clients surface them (user selection vs. model invocation).
background: MCP defines three server primitives: Resources (context/data), Tools (executable functions for the model), and Prompts (templated messages and workflows for users). The key distinction is invocation intent: Tools are called by the AI model during reasoning, while Prompts are selected by the user to structure an interaction. This separation guides how MCP clients present these capabilities in their UI.
options: Prompts are templated messages and workflows designed for user-driven interactions, while Tools are functions designed for model-driven execution
Prompts and Tools are interchangeable — the distinction is purely cosmetic for client UI purposes
Prompts are system instructions injected into every request, while Tools are user-visible UI elements
Prompts execute arbitrary code on the server, while Tools only return static data

text: A monorepo has a root CLAUDE.md and a packages/api/CLAUDE.md. A developer runs Claude Code from the packages/api/ directory. Which configuration files are loaded and in what order?
explanation: Correct: Claude Code walks up the directory tree, loading CLAUDE.md files from ancestors, then applies the nearest (child) file with highest precedence. Distractor 1 is wrong because Claude Code loads the full ancestor chain, not just the CWD file. Distractor 2 is wrong because child files override ancestors on conflict, not the reverse. Distractor 3 is wrong because loading follows directory hierarchy, not alphabetical order.
background: Claude Code loads CLAUDE.md configuration by walking up from the current working directory to the repository root, collecting all CLAUDE.md files in the ancestor chain. Instructions are merged with child-level files taking precedence over parent-level files on conflicts. This enables teams to set broad conventions at the repo root while allowing subdirectories to specialize or override as needed.
options: Both files are loaded but merged alphabetically by filename, with no guaranteed precedence
Only the packages/api/CLAUDE.md is loaded, since Claude Code only reads the CLAUDE.md in the current working directory
Both files are loaded — the root CLAUDE.md first, then the packages/api/CLAUDE.md, with the child file's instructions taking precedence on conflicts
Only the root CLAUDE.md is loaded, since ancestor files always override child files

text: In a multi-agent system using Claude, a subagent encounters a tool that returns an HTTP 500 error. According to agentic reliability best practices, what should the subagent do?
explanation: Correct: Bounded retries handle transient errors, and structured error reporting lets the coordinator make an informed decision. Distractor 1 is wrong because a single tool failure should not terminate the entire workflow — graceful degradation is preferred. Distractor 2 is wrong because silently swallowing errors leads to corrupt results and violates transparency. Distractor 3 is wrong because unbounded retries risk infinite loops and resource exhaustion.
background: Agentic reliability patterns distinguish between transient errors (worth retrying) and persistent failures (requiring escalation). The recommended approach is bounded retries with exponential backoff for transient errors, followed by structured error propagation to the coordinator if retries are exhausted. The coordinator can then decide to skip, substitute, or fail the overall task with full context.
options: Immediately terminate the entire multi-agent workflow, since any tool failure indicates an unrecoverable state
Retry with a bounded retry count, and if retries are exhausted, return a structured error to the coordinator with failure context rather than hallucinating a result
Silently skip the failed tool and continue with partial data, reporting success to the coordinator
Retry indefinitely until the tool succeeds, since the coordinator has no mechanism to handle partial failures

text: You want Claude to produce deterministic JSON output matching a precise schema. Which approach provides the strongest schema enforcement guarantee?
explanation: Correct: When Claude calls a tool, the input must conform to the tool's JSON schema, providing API-level enforcement. Distractor 1 relies on prompt compliance, which is not guaranteed — the model may deviate. Distractor 2 improves likelihood but does not enforce the schema programmatically. Distractor 3 is fabricated — there is no 'response format' header in the Messages API.
background: Tool use provides the strongest schema enforcement for structured output from Claude. By defining the desired output shape as a tool's input_schema, developers leverage the API's built-in JSON schema validation. The model must produce conforming JSON to successfully 'call' the tool, making this approach more reliable than prompt-based instructions alone.
options: Define the output schema as a tool's input_schema and have Claude return the structured data by calling the tool
Append a few-shot example of the desired JSON at the end of the user message
Use a system prompt instructing Claude to 'always respond with valid JSON matching the following schema'
Set the response format to 'application/json' in the API request headers

text: An agentic application's context window is approaching capacity during a complex multi-step task. The orchestrator needs to continue the task without losing critical state. What is the recommended production approach?
explanation: Correct: Compaction summarizes older turns to free space while preserving essential state, allowing the task to continue. Distractor 1 loses all accumulated task context and intermediate results. Distractor 2 is wrong because max_tokens controls output length, not the context window size. Distractor 3 is impractical mid-conversation and may introduce behavioural inconsistencies.
background: Context compaction is the production pattern for managing context window exhaustion in long-running agentic tasks. The orchestrator summarizes completed conversation turns (preserving key decisions, intermediate results, and active constraints) while keeping the system prompt and recent turns intact. This is preferable to truncation, which loses structured state unpredictably.
options: Increase the model's max_tokens parameter to expand the available context window
Use context compaction to summarize completed conversation turns while preserving the system prompt and active task state
Switch to a model with a larger context window mid-conversation to accommodate the overflow
Start a brand new conversation and include only the original system prompt, discarding all prior context

text: According to MCP design principles, servers should be 'extremely easy to build.' Which architectural decision most directly supports this principle?
