explanation: Correct: MCP explicitly states that host applications handle complex orchestration, enabling servers to remain simple and focused. Distractor 1 is wrong because authentication and context aggregation are host responsibilities, not server requirements. Distractor 2 is wrong because conversation history stays with the host — servers receive only necessary context. Distractor 3 is wrong because servers are isolated and do not coordinate directly with each other.
background: MCP's design philosophy deliberately pushes complexity to the host layer so that server developers face minimal implementation burden. Servers focus on exposing specific, well-defined capabilities through simple interfaces. The host handles orchestration, security enforcement, context aggregation, and cross-server coordination. This asymmetry is key to MCP's rapid ecosystem growth.
options: Host applications handle complex orchestration responsibilities, so servers can focus on simple, well-defined capabilities
Servers must coordinate directly with other servers to handle cross-cutting concerns
Servers are required to maintain their own conversation history to reduce host complexity
Servers must implement the full protocol stack including authentication, context aggregation, and model sampling

text: In a production agentic system, Claude calls a tool and receives a very large result (e.g., 50,000 tokens of raw database output). What is the recommended pattern to prevent context window waste?
explanation: Correct: Code execution lets the agent filter large results before they consume context tokens — this is the core insight of the code-execution-with-MCP pattern. Distractor 1 wastes context and may cause truncation in subsequent turns. Distractor 2 loses data indiscriminately and may remove critical information. Distractor 3 is absurd — the model needs data to reason correctly.
background: When agents interact with data-rich tools, raw results can consume vast amounts of context. The recommended pattern is to use code execution (e.g., a sandboxed environment) to filter, aggregate, or transform results before they enter the model's context window. Anthropic's engineering blog reports up to 98.7% token reduction using this approach compared to passing raw tool results directly.
options: Cache the full result server-side and never return any data to the model, asking it to guess the answer
Configure the tool to always return results truncated to 1,000 tokens, regardless of the query
Use code execution to filter, aggregate, or transform the raw tool result in-environment before it enters the model's context window
Pass the full 50,000-token result into the context and rely on Claude to extract relevant portions in its response

text: An MCP client application needs to prevent personally identifiable information (PII) from reaching the language model. According to MCP security best practices, where should PII filtering occur?
explanation: Correct: The MCP client sits between the server and the model, making it the ideal interception point for PII before model exposure. Distractor 1 is wrong because you may not control all servers, and server-side filtering doesn't protect against PII in server responses. Distractor 2 is wrong because prompt-based filtering is unreliable — the model has already seen the PII. Distractor 3 is too blunt and may break legitimate protocol traffic.
background: In MCP's architecture, the client is the component that mediates between servers and the host/model. Security best practices recommend that PII interception and tokenization happen at the client layer, before data reaches the language model. This ensures that even if a server returns sensitive data, it is sanitized before model exposure. This is a defense-in-depth approach complementing server-side controls.
options: At the network layer using a reverse proxy that strips PII patterns from all HTTP traffic
At the MCP client layer, intercepting and tokenizing PII before the data is sent to the model
At the MCP server layer, since servers control all data that enters the protocol
In the model's system prompt, instructing it to ignore and not repeat any PII it encounters

text: An agent must process a 180-page PDF contract and answer detailed questions about it. The document fits within Claude's context window, but accuracy on details in the middle sections is lower than expected. What is the most effective mitigation?
explanation: Correct: LLMs exhibit primacy and recency bias — content at the start and end of context receives more attention. Strategically positioning critical sections mitigates the 'lost in the middle' effect. Distractor 1 destroys cross-page context needed for contract analysis. Distractor 2 has negligible impact — system prompt length is not the bottleneck. Distractor 3 is wrong because temperature controls sampling randomness, not attention distribution across context.
background: Research on long-context LLMs shows a 'lost in the middle' phenomenon where information in the middle of a long context receives less attention than content at the beginning or end. For production applications processing long documents, strategic content positioning — placing the most critical sections near context boundaries — is an effective mitigation alongside techniques like targeted extraction and section-based querying.
options: Set temperature to 0 to eliminate all variability in how the model attends to middle sections
Split the PDF into 180 separate API calls, one per page, and aggregate the answers
Extract and re-order relevant sections to place critical content near the beginning and end of the context, leveraging primacy and recency effects
Reduce the system prompt to under 50 tokens so the model allocates more attention to the document

text: An MCP server needs to expose a curated list of company FAQs that an AI assistant can reference when answering customer questions. The data is read-only and changes infrequently. Which MCP primitive is the most appropriate choice?
pattern: Static Data as MCP Resource Primitive
explanation: Resources are the correct primitive for exposing read-only contextual data. Tools are for executable functions that perform actions or computations — overkill for static data. Prompts are user-facing templated workflows, not data containers. Sampling is a client feature that lets servers request LLM completions, unrelated to data exposure.
background: MCP defines three server primitives: Resources (context/data), Tools (executable functions), and Prompts (templated workflows). Resources are ideal for static or infrequently-changing data that the model needs as context, while Tools should be reserved for operations that have side effects or require computation.
options: Tools — the server should expose a getFrequentlyAskedQuestions() tool the model calls on demand
Sampling — the server should request the client to generate summaries of each FAQ on load
Prompts — each FAQ should be wrapped as a templated prompt the user selects
Resources — they are designed for exposing read-only contextual data to the model or user

text: A multi-agent system has a coordinator that delegates tasks to three specialist subagents. One subagent fails due to a transient API timeout. What is the recommended production pattern for handling this?
explanation: The coordinator pattern requires subagents to report structured errors so the coordinator retains decision authority. Silent indefinite retries risk hanging the system. Terminating all subagents for one failure violates graceful degradation. Returning empty success hides the failure, corrupting downstream results.
background: In multi-agent orchestration, the coordinator must remain the central decision-maker for error handling. Subagents should propagate structured error information (not raw exceptions) so the coordinator can apply retry policies, partial-completion strategies, or human escalation as appropriate.
options: The subagent reports a structured error back to the coordinator, which decides whether to retry, skip, or escalate to a human
The subagent returns an empty success result so the coordinator can continue without interruption
The subagent silently retries indefinitely until the API recovers
The coordinator catches the exception at the process level and terminates all subagents

text: A developer notices that Claude occasionally ignores instructions placed in the middle of a very long system prompt. Which well-documented phenomenon explains this behavior?
explanation: This is the well-known primacy-recency (U-shaped attention) effect where models attend more strongly to content at the start and end of long contexts. Token overflow would produce a stop_reason of max_tokens, not selective ignoring. Prompt caching is a billing optimization that doesn't change how the model reads content. Schema enforcement is not a real attention mechanism.
background: Large language models exhibit attention patterns where information at the beginning and end of a prompt receives stronger attention than content in the middle. This is especially pronounced in very long system prompts. Production mitigations include placing critical instructions at the start or end, using strategic repetition, and re-injecting key instructions periodically in long sessions.
options: Token overflow — the instructions exceed the context window and are silently truncated
Prompt caching — cached system prompts skip re-reading middle sections on subsequent turns
Attention decay — models attend more strongly to the beginning and end of long prompts, with weaker attention to the middle
Schema enforcement — only structured output instructions receive full model attention

text: An engineering team connects their agent to 15 MCP servers exposing a combined 400 tools. Response latency has increased significantly. According to Anthropic's guidance, what is the primary architectural remedy?
pattern: Code Execution for Tool Definition Scale
explanation: Anthropic's code-execution-with-MCP pattern addresses exactly this: agents write code to call tools rather than loading all definitions into context, achieving up to 98.7% token reduction. Increasing max_tokens doesn't reduce input token bloat from definitions. Merging servers violates the composability and simplicity principles. Transport choice doesn't affect the number of tool definitions loaded into context.
background: When agents connect to many MCP servers with hundreds of tools, loading all tool definitions upfront consumes massive context. Anthropic's recommended pattern is code execution: the agent writes code against MCP server file-based APIs, using progressive tool discovery (search_tools with detail levels) to load only needed schemas on demand.
options: Increase the model's max_tokens parameter to give it more space to process tool definitions
Use code execution so the agent writes code against MCP server APIs, loading tool schemas on demand rather than injecting all 400 definitions upfront
Merge all 15 MCP servers into a single server to reduce connection overhead
Switch from Streamable HTTP transport to stdio transport for all servers

text: A monorepo has a root CLAUDE.md and a services/payments/CLAUDE.md. A developer runs Claude Code from the services/payments/ directory. Which statement is true about the configuration that Claude sees?
explanation: CLAUDE.md files follow ancestor chain loading: Claude walks up the directory tree and loads each CLAUDE.md it finds, with child files taking precedence on conflicts. The child doesn't suppress the parent — both are active. The root alone being loaded contradicts ancestor chain behavior. Parent-takes-precedence is the reverse of the actual rule.
background: Claude Code loads CLAUDE.md files from every ancestor directory up to the project root, creating a layered configuration. This enables monorepos to define shared conventions at the root while individual services override or extend specific settings. In case of conflicting instructions, the most specific (deepest) CLAUDE.md wins.
options: Claude loads the root CLAUDE.md first, then layers the services/payments/CLAUDE.md on top — both are active with the child taking precedence on conflicts
Only the services/payments/CLAUDE.md is loaded because the working directory's file overrides all ancestors
Only the root CLAUDE.md is loaded because it is the project-level configuration
Both files are loaded but the root CLAUDE.md takes precedence on any conflicting instructions

text: You need Claude to extract structured data from invoices and guarantee the output is valid JSON matching a specific schema. Which approach provides the strongest guarantee of schema conformance?
explanation: Using a tool's input_schema forces the API to validate the output against the JSON schema at the protocol level — the strongest guarantee available. Few-shot examples in prompts provide guidance but no enforcement. Regex post-processing is fragile and can't guarantee structural validity. There is no 'json' response format header in the Claude API.
background: The tool-use mechanism in Claude's API accepts an input_schema (JSON Schema) that the model's output must conform to. By defining a 'dummy' tool whose schema matches the desired output structure, developers get API-level enforcement of valid JSON output — stronger than prompt-based approaches alone.
options: Include a JSON example in the system prompt and ask Claude to follow the same format
Define the output schema as a tool's input_schema and instruct Claude to 'call' that tool — the API enforces the schema at the protocol level
Set the response format to 'json' in the API request headers and parse the result
Use a regex-based post-processing step to fix any JSON formatting errors in Claude's text output

text: In the MCP architecture, which component is responsible for enforcing user consent requirements before allowing a server to execute a sensitive tool?
pattern: Host as Consent and Policy Authority
explanation: The MCP specification explicitly assigns security policy enforcement, user consent, and authorization to the host. Servers are isolated and should not interact with users directly for consent. Clients route messages but don't independently make policy decisions — they act on behalf of the host. The registry is an index of available servers, not a runtime policy enforcer.
background: In MCP's client-host-server architecture, the host is the security authority. It creates and manages clients, controls their connection permissions, enforces security policies, and handles user authorization decisions. This centralizes trust at the host level, keeping servers simple and isolated.
options: The MCP client — it intercepts tool calls and displays consent dialogs independently
The host — it controls security policies, user authorization, and consent requirements
The MCP registry — it marks tools as sensitive during server registration and blocks unauthorized calls
The MCP server — it must prompt the user for consent before executing any sensitive operation

text: During a long agentic session, the context window is approaching its limit. The agent needs to continue working. What is the recommended approach according to Anthropic's documentation?
explanation: Compaction — summarizing completed work while retaining active context — is the documented approach for managing long sessions. Replaying all tool calls wastes tokens and may produce stale results. max_tokens controls output length, not the context window size. Switching models mid-session breaks continuity and isn't a supported mid-conversation operation.
background: When agentic sessions approach context window limits, compaction preserves the most relevant information by summarizing completed steps and discarding intermediate results. This allows the agent to continue operating effectively without losing critical context about its goals and progress.
options: Switch to a model with a larger context window mid-session to avoid any summarization
Increase the max_tokens parameter to expand the available context window
Immediately start a new session and replay all previous tool calls from scratch
Compact the conversation by summarizing completed work and retaining only active context, then continue in the same session

text: An agentic loop receives a response from Claude with stop_reason: "tool_use". What should the agent do next?
explanation: stop_reason: tool_use means Claude wants to call a tool and is waiting for the result. The agent must execute the tool and return the result in the next message. Returning it to the user as a final answer breaks the agentic loop. tool_use is not an error — it's a normal control flow signal. Re-appending tool definitions and resending would restart the conversation unnecessarily.
background: In Claude's API, the stop_reason field signals why the model stopped generating. 'tool_use' indicates the model is requesting a tool call as part of its reasoning. The agentic loop pattern requires the orchestrator to execute the tool and feed the result back, continuing until it receives 'end_turn' (task complete) or 'max_tokens' (truncation).
options: Append the tool definition to the system prompt and resend the original user message
Return the tool_use response directly to the end user as the final answer
Execute the requested tool, then send a new message containing the tool result so Claude can continue processing
Retry the same request because tool_use indicates the model encountered an error

text: The MCP specification states that each MCP client maintains a 1:1 relationship with a server. A host application needs to connect to five different MCP servers. How many MCP client instances must the host create?
pattern: One Client Per Server Instance Rule
explanation: The MCP specification explicitly states each client has a 1:1 relationship with a server, so five servers require five client instances. A single multiplexing client would violate server isolation. The 1:1 rule applies regardless of transport type. Grouping by transport contradicts the specification's per-server isolation model.
background: MCP enforces a 1:1 client-server relationship to maintain isolation between servers. Each client establishes one stateful session with one server, handles protocol negotiation independently, and maintains security boundaries. The host creates and manages all client instances, ensuring no server can see into another's session.
options: Five — one dedicated client instance per server connection
Two — one client for local servers (stdio) and one for remote servers (HTTP)
One — a single client multiplexes connections to all five servers
It depends on the transport — stdio needs one per server, but HTTP can share a client

text: A team wants to run a custom linter automatically every time Claude Code writes to a .py file. Which hook event should they configure?
explanation: PostToolUse fires after a tool completes, letting you inspect the result and run validation. By combining a tool matcher (Write) with a glob path matcher (*.py), you target only Python file writes. PreToolUse fires before execution so the file doesn't exist yet—linting would fail. Stop fires at conversation end, not per-file. Notification is for user-facing messages, not file change detection.
background: Claude Code hooks support matcher patterns that filter by tool name and input fields like file paths. PostToolUse is the canonical event for post-write validation because the file content is already on disk and available for external tools like linters or formatters.
options: Stop — to validate all written files once the agent finishes its turn
Notification — which fires whenever a file change is detected
PreToolUse — to intercept the write before it reaches the filesystem
PostToolUse — with a matcher filtering for the Write tool and *.py file paths

text: In a multi-agent system, the coordinator dispatches a task to a subagent that encounters an unrecoverable error. According to Anthropic's recommended orchestration patterns, what should the subagent do?
explanation: Subagents should surface failures as structured data back to the coordinator, which has the broader context to decide whether to retry, reroute, or escalate. Indefinite retries can cause infinite loops. Subagents should not bypass the coordinator to contact users directly—that violates the hub-and-spoke communication model. Swallowing errors silently produces incorrect downstream results.
background: In agentic architectures, the coordinator is the decision-making authority. Subagents are specialists with limited scope. When a subagent fails, escalating the structured error preserves the coordinator's ability to apply cross-cutting recovery logic such as fallback to another subagent or human-in-the-loop escalation.
options: Retry the task indefinitely until it succeeds, since subagents should be self-healing
Silently swallow the error and return an empty success response to avoid disrupting the workflow
Return a structured error result to the coordinator, which decides the next recovery step
Directly notify the end user about the failure and ask for corrective input

text: An MCP server exposes a get_user_profile tool and a user://profile/{id} resource, both returning the same data. A client needs to display a user profile in a read-only dashboard. Which should it use and why?
explanation: MCP resources are designed for read-only context exposure and signal to the client that no mutation occurs. Tools imply model-triggered actions with potential side effects. Resources can return structured data just fine. Tools are not inherently faster. MCP explicitly distinguishes three primitive types (tools, resources, prompts), so they are not interchangeable.
background: MCP's resource primitive is purpose-built for exposing data that the model or application reads without side effects. Choosing the correct primitive communicates intent: resources are safe to prefetch and cache, while tools require explicit invocation consent and may modify state.
options: The resource, because resources represent read-only contextual data and carry no side-effect semantics
The tool, because tools are always faster than resources for data retrieval
Either one interchangeably — MCP treats tools and resources as identical primitives
The tool, because resources cannot return structured JSON data

text: You need Claude to classify customer support tickets into one of five categories with a JSON output. During testing, you notice Claude occasionally adds a "reasoning" field not in your schema. What is the most reliable fix?
pattern: Schema Strictness via Tool Input Validation
explanation: Tool use with input_schema provides API-level enforcement: Claude must conform to the JSON Schema, so extraneous fields are structurally impossible. Prompt instructions reduce but do not eliminate extra fields—they are probabilistic. Post-processing is a workaround, not prevention. XML has no structural enforcement either and can contain arbitrary tags.
background: When you define a tool's input_schema, the API validates Claude's output against it before returning the response. This is the strongest guarantee of structural conformity available, turning a probabilistic compliance problem into a deterministic one.
options: Use tool use with an input_schema that defines only the allowed fields, enforcing the schema at the API level
Switch to XML output format, which does not allow extra tags
Add "Do not include any extra fields" to the system prompt and rely on instruction-following
Post-process the JSON response to strip unknown keys before passing it downstream

text: A developer places critical safety instructions only in the system prompt of a 180k-token conversation. After many turns, Claude occasionally ignores these instructions. What phenomenon explains this, and what is the recommended mitigation?
explanation: In very long conversations, content far from the current generation point receives less attention (primacy-recency effect). The system prompt is not dropped or truncated by the API at any token threshold. Safety training does not override custom instructions in this manner. The fix is periodic re-injection of critical instructions at strategic points in the conversation to keep them in high-attention zones.
background: Claude processes the full context window, but empirically, instructions positioned far from the most recent turns can lose influence in very long sessions. Re-injecting key instructions—especially safety-critical ones—near the end of the context or after summarization compaction events ensures they remain salient.
options: Claude's safety training overrides custom system prompts at long context lengths — file a support ticket to allowlist the instructions
The system prompt is dropped after 100k tokens — move instructions to the first user message instead
Token budget exhaustion causes the system prompt to be silently truncated — reduce the system prompt to under 500 tokens
Attention decay on distant system prompt content — re-inject the safety instructions periodically in user turns or at summarization boundaries

text: A hook is configured with "type": "command" and exits with code 2 on the PreToolUse event. What happens?
explanation: In Claude Code hooks, exit code 2 has special semantics: it signals a decision to block the action. For PreToolUse, this prevents the tool from executing and feeds the hook's stderr back to the model so it can adjust. Exit code 0 means proceed, exit code 1 means the hook itself errored. There is no automatic retry mechanism for hook-blocked actions.
background: Claude Code hooks use a three-tier exit code convention: 0 = allow/success, 1 = hook error (logged but action proceeds), 2 = decision block (action is prevented). Exit code 2 on PreToolUse is the mechanism for programmatic tool call rejection, such as blocking writes to protected files.
options: Claude Code crashes with an unhandled hook error and the session terminates
The tool call is retried automatically up to three times before failing
The tool call proceeds normally — exit code 2 is treated as a success
The tool call is blocked and the hook's stderr output is shown to Claude as feedback

text: You are designing an agentic loop that uses tools. Claude returns a response with stop_reason: "max_tokens" after a tool-use turn. What does this indicate and how should your code handle it?
pattern: Max Tokens Truncation in Tool Loops
explanation: stop_reason 'max_tokens' means the response hit the token ceiling before Claude finished generating. A tool_use block may be incomplete and unparseable. You should not attempt to parse partial JSON. The context window limit produces a different error (not a stop_reason). 'end_turn' signals loop termination, not 'max_tokens'. The correct recovery is to increase max_tokens or send a continuation prompt.
background: In agentic loops, truncation is a reliability hazard because partial tool_use JSON cannot be executed. Production systems should monitor stop_reason on every response and implement truncation recovery—either by raising max_tokens or by appending a continuation message asking Claude to complete its response.
options: Claude is signaling that it wants to end the agentic loop — stop processing
Claude has finished its turn and is waiting for the tool result — parse the tool call and execute it
Claude's response was truncated mid-generation — resend the conversation with a higher max_tokens value or prompt Claude to continue
The conversation has exceeded the context window — summarize and restart

text: An MCP server needs to ask the user a clarifying question during a tool execution (e.g., "Which AWS region should I deploy to?"). Which MCP primitive is designed for this?
explanation: Elicitation is the MCP mechanism for servers to request user input during execution, subject to host consent policies. Sampling allows servers to request LLM completions, not user input. Resources are read-only data, not interactive. Prompts are user-triggered templates that populate tool calls — they flow from user to server, not server to user.
background: MCP elicitation solves the problem of servers needing runtime user input without breaking the security model. The host mediates all elicitation requests, can modify or reject them, and ensures the user's consent. This keeps the server from having direct user access while still enabling interactive workflows.
options: Prompts — which are user-initiated templates, not server-initiated requests
Resources — which expose a URI the user can navigate to for input
Elicitation — which allows a server to request structured user input through the client and host
Sampling — which lets the server call the LLM to generate a user-facing prompt

text: You are designing a classification prompt where 95% of inputs fall into known categories, but 5% are genuinely ambiguous. Your JSON schema defines an enum for the category field. What should you add to handle ambiguous inputs reliably?
pattern: Enum Escape Value for Low-Confidence Classification
explanation: Adding an explicit escape value like 'other' keeps the enum constraint intact while giving Claude a valid output for genuinely ambiguous inputs. Null conflates missing data with uncertainty. Removing the enum loses all structural guarantees. A separate boolean still forces a potentially wrong category choice, which corrupts downstream logic that branches on category values.
background: Classification schemas should anticipate edge cases. An explicit escape enum value is preferable to null because it distinguishes 'the model evaluated and found ambiguity' from 'the field was not populated.' This pattern is especially important in production pipelines where downstream systems switch on category values.
options: Omit the enum constraint entirely so Claude can write free-text explanations for edge cases
Allow Claude to return the category field as null, signaling uncertainty implicitly
Add an "other" or "uncertain" value to the enum and instruct Claude to use it when confidence is below a threshold
Add a separate boolean "is_ambiguous" field and still require a category pick

text: An agent is in a long-running session and triggers a compaction event to free context space. A PreCompact hook is configured. What can this hook do?
explanation: PreCompact fires before compaction begins, allowing hooks to append content that will be included in the summarization input — ensuring critical context (like active constraints or state) survives compaction. Hooks cannot cancel compaction, manually select messages for removal, or replace the compaction mechanism with an external service. These are not supported hook decision controls for PreCompact.
background: Context compaction in Claude Code summarizes the conversation to reclaim token space. The PreCompact hook gives developers a last chance to inject must-preserve information into the context before summarization occurs. This is critical for long-running agents where certain invariants (e.g., security rules, active task state) must persist across compaction boundaries.
options: Cancel the compaction entirely and force Claude to continue with the full context
Choose which specific messages to remove from the conversation history
Redirect the compaction to an external summarization service instead of Claude's built-in one
Inject additional instructions or context that must survive the compaction summarization

text: A DevOps team needs to enforce a security policy across all developers using Claude Code on company machines, ensuring no individual developer can override it. Which configuration scope should they use?
explanation: Managed scope is explicitly designed for 'security policies that must be enforced organization-wide' and 'compliance requirements that can't be overridden,' deployed by IT/DevOps. Project scope is shared but can be overridden and requires repo access. User scope is personal and not centrally managed. Local scope is per-user per-project and gitignored.
background: Claude Code's configuration system has four scopes: Managed, User, Project, and Local. Managed scope sits at the highest precedence for enforcement and is deployed via system-level mechanisms (plist on macOS, registry on Windows, or managed-settings.json), making it ideal for organization-wide security policies that individual developers cannot override.
options: User scope, placed in each developer's ~/.claude/ directory
Project scope, committed to the repository's .claude/ directory
Managed scope, deployed via system-level managed-settings.json or plist/registry
Local scope, using .claude/settings.local.json on each machine

text: You are building a classification pipeline where Claude must assign one of 15 categories to each input. During testing, you notice Claude occasionally invents category names not in your list. What is the most reliable fix?
explanation: Tool input schemas with enum fields provide API-level enforcement — Claude literally cannot return a value outside the enum. System prompt instructions help but are not guaranteed, especially at scale. Few-shot examples teach patterns but don't prevent novel outputs. Temperature 0 reduces randomness but doesn't structurally prevent out-of-vocabulary outputs.
background: When classification must be restricted to a closed set, prompt-level instructions are soft constraints that degrade under edge cases. Tool use with enum-typed input schemas provides a hard, schema-enforced constraint at the API level, making it the production-safe approach for fixed-category classification.
options: Define a tool whose input schema uses an enum field restricted to the 15 valid categories, forcing Claude to select from the list
Add a system prompt instruction saying 'Only use categories from the following list' and list all 15
Use temperature 0 to eliminate randomness and prevent hallucinated categories
Provide 15 few-shot examples, one per category, so Claude learns the valid set

text: A multi-turn customer support agent accumulates long conversation histories. After ~80 turns, users report that Claude ignores instructions from early in the conversation. Which cognitive bias explains this degradation?
explanation: In long contexts, the model's attention concentrates on recent content (recency bias) and the very beginning (primacy bias), causing middle and early-conversation instructions to lose influence. Token budget exhaustion is a different issue — the context window is large but attention is not uniform within it. There is no 'system prompt override' mechanism, and temperature does not drift during a conversation.
background: Large language models exhibit primacy-recency attention patterns: content at the beginning and end of context receives disproportionate attention, while material in the middle gets less focus. In long multi-turn conversations, early instructions can effectively fade from influence, which is why techniques like periodic instruction re-injection are recommended.
options: Token budget exhaustion — the model physically cannot read tokens beyond a fixed limit
Temperature drift — extended conversations cause the effective temperature to increase over time
System prompt override — user messages automatically supersede system-level instructions after 50 turns
Attention concentration on recent turns (recency bias), causing early instructions to receive less model attention

text: An MCP server exposes a get_customer_profile tool that returns a full customer record including SSN, address, and purchase history. The AI application only needs purchase history. According to MCP best practices, where should the unnecessary PII be stripped?
pattern: Minimal Data Exposure at Server Boundary
explanation: The MCP Server Simplicity Principle and least-privilege design dictate that servers should only expose the data needed for the use case. Returning unnecessary PII and relying on downstream filtering (model instructions, host parsing, or transport-level hacks) creates unnecessary risk. Once PII enters the context window, prompt-level instructions cannot guarantee it won't appear in outputs. Transport-level JSON filtering is fragile and not a supported MCP mechanism.
background: In MCP architecture, the server is the data boundary. Best practice is to design tool responses that return only the minimum data required for the task. This follows defense-in-depth: rather than trusting downstream components to filter sensitive data, prevent it from entering the pipeline at all.
options: At the MCP server level — the tool should only return the fields the AI application needs
At the host level — parse and redact the tool result before passing it to the client
At the model level — instruct Claude in the system prompt to ignore PII fields in tool results
At the transport level — configure the stdio pipe to filter JSON fields matching PII patterns

text: An orchestrator agent delegates a database migration task to a subagent. The subagent completes 3 of 5 migration steps, then encounters a permissions error on step 4. What should the subagent return to the orchestrator?
explanation: Structured partial-progress reporting gives the orchestrator maximum information to decide next steps — it knows what succeeded (no need to re-run), what failed (can fix permissions and retry just step 4), and what remains. A simple error string loses completed-step context. Autonomous retry with elevated permissions violates least privilege. A boolean false forces the orchestrator to re-run everything, wasting completed work.
background: In multi-agent systems, subagent failures rarely mean total failure. Production-grade orchestration requires subagents to report partial progress so the coordinator can make informed recovery decisions — retry the failed step, escalate to a human, or skip and continue. This minimizes wasted work and enables surgical error recovery.
options: Nothing — the subagent should retry with elevated permissions autonomously until it succeeds
A simple error string: 'Permission denied on step 4' so the orchestrator can retry
A boolean false indicating failure, letting the orchestrator re-run the entire task
A structured result listing the 3 completed steps, the failed step with the error detail, and the 2 remaining steps not attempted

text: A team commits shared MCP server configurations and permission rules in the .claude/ directory of their repository. A developer wants to add a personal MCP server for local testing without affecting teammates. Where should they place this configuration?
pattern: Local Scope for Personal Project Overrides
explanation: Local scope (.claude/settings.local.json) is specifically designed for 'personal overrides for a specific project' and is gitignored so it won't affect teammates. Project-level settings.json is committed and shared — adding personal config there would push to all collaborators. Managed scope is for IT-deployed org-wide policies. User scope (~/.claude/) would apply the MCP server to all projects, not just this one.
background: Claude Code's four configuration scopes enable clean separation between shared team settings and personal preferences. The Local scope (.claude/settings.local.json) is the designated location for per-user, per-project customizations that should not be version-controlled or shared with collaborators.

refs.label: Claude Code Settings — When to Use Each Scope

options: The managed scope via managed-settings.json on their machine
.claude/settings.local.json, which is gitignored and applies only to that user in that project
.claude/settings.json in the project directory, with a comment marking it as personal
~/.claude/settings.json in their home directory, which applies to all their projects

text: You need Claude to extract a list of action items from meeting notes. Some meetings have no action items. What output format best handles the zero-result case without ambiguity?
explanation: An empty array maintains type consistency — downstream code can always iterate over action_items without type-checking. A human-readable string requires string-matching to detect the zero case and breaks typed parsing. Returning null forces null-checking and is ambiguous (does null mean 'none found' or 'extraction failed'?). Omitting the field requires key-existence checks and is the most error-prone approach.
background: In structured extraction pipelines, consistent schema shapes across all outputs — including edge cases — is critical for reliability. The empty collection pattern (returning [] instead of null, sentinel strings, or omitted fields) ensures downstream consumers can process results uniformly without branching on type or existence checks.
options: Return the string "No action items found" so the consumer gets a clear human-readable message
Omit the action_items field entirely from the response when there are none
Return null to indicate the absence of action items
Return an object with an "action_items" array field that is empty ([]) when no items are found

text: You are designing an agentic system where Claude must interact with 25 different tools. Testing shows Claude frequently selects the wrong tool. Which architectural change most directly improves tool selection accuracy?
explanation: Research and practice show that tool selection accuracy degrades as the number of available tools increases. Decomposing into subagents with smaller, domain-scoped tool sets directly reduces the selection space. Increasing temperature would make selection less deterministic, not more accurate. Retry loops add latency without addressing the root cause. Numeric prefixes don't help the model understand tool semantics.
background: When an agent has access to many tools, the model must discriminate between increasingly similar options, leading to selection errors. The recommended pattern is to use a coordinator that routes to specialized subagents, each with a focused tool set. This reduces cognitive load per decision and improves reliability.
options: Add a retry loop that re-prompts Claude if the wrong tool is detected
Increase the model's temperature to encourage more diverse tool exploration
Group tools into domain-specific subagents so each agent sees only 5–8 relevant tools
Rename all tools with numeric prefixes (tool_01, tool_02, …) for deterministic ordering

text: An agentic loop has been running for 50 iterations, and the context window is at 85% capacity. The agent needs to continue processing. What should the system do before the context is completely full?
explanation: Proactive compaction preserves the essential state (what was accomplished, current goals) while reclaiming token budget for continued work. Switching models mid-loop is operationally complex and may change behavior. Immediate termination wastes completed work. Silent truncation from the beginning risks losing system prompts and critical early instructions, potentially causing the agent to lose its purpose.
background: Long-running agentic loops accumulate large volumes of intermediate tool results and reasoning. Rather than waiting for the context window to overflow (which causes hard failures or silent truncation), production systems should monitor context usage and trigger compaction at a threshold (e.g., 80%), summarizing completed work while preserving active goals and state.
options: Proactively compact the conversation by summarizing completed work and dropping raw tool results that are no longer needed
Silently truncate the oldest messages from the beginning of the conversation
Switch to a model with a larger context window and replay the entire conversation
Immediately terminate the agent loop and return whatever partial results exist

text: An MCP server currently exposes a prompts/generate_report prompt template and a tools/run_query tool. A developer proposes converting the prompt into a tool so Claude can invoke it autonomously. Why is this change problematic?
explanation: MCP distinguishes between prompts (user-initiated template workflows) and tools (model-initiated actions). Prompts represent pre-built interaction patterns a human chooses to activate; tools are capabilities the model decides to use autonomously. Converting a prompt to a tool changes the invocation authority from human to model, which may not be appropriate for report generation workflows. Tools absolutely can accept string inputs. There is no performance difference based on primitive type. Servers can expose all three primitive types simultaneously.
background: MCP defines three server primitives: Resources (read-only data for context), Tools (model-invoked actions with side effects), and Prompts (user-initiated interaction templates). The distinction between Prompts and Tools is primarily about invocation intent — who decides to trigger the action. Prompts are selected by users through the host UI; tools are selected by the model during agentic reasoning.
options: MCP servers are limited to exposing either prompts or tools, not both simultaneously
Tools cannot accept string templates as input parameters in the MCP specification
Prompts execute faster than tools because they skip the schema validation step
Prompts are designed for user-driven workflows where a human selects the template; converting to a tool removes the user-initiation intent
